      "code": "% PRISM-3D: A&A Letter\n% Using emulateapj as A&A class proxy (two-column, journal-style)\n\\documentclass[twocolumn,letterpaper]{article}\n\n% \u2500\u2500 Packages \u2500\u2500\n\\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}\n\\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}\n\\usepackage{graphicx}\n\\usepackage{natbib}\n\\usepackage{hyperref}\n\\usepackage{xcolor}\n\\usepackage{booktabs}\n\\usepackage{microtype}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta,positioning,shapes.geometric,fit,calc}\n\n% \u2500\u2500 Page geometry (A&A Letter style) \u2500\u2500\n\\usepackage[top=2.2cm,bottom=2.5cm,left=1.8cm,right=1.8cm]{geometry}\n\\setlength{\\columnsep}{0.6cm}\n\n% \u2500\u2500 Journal-style formatting \u2500\u2500\n\\renewcommand{\\abstractname}{\\textsc{Abstract}}\n\\newcommand{\\aap}{A\\&A}\n\\newcommand{\\apj}{ApJ}\n\\newcommand{\\mnras}{MNRAS}\n\\newcommand{\\zetacr}{\\zeta_{\\rm CR}}\n\\newcommand{\\nH}{n_{\\rm H}}\n\\newcommand{\\Tgas}{T_{\\rm gas}}\n\\newcommand{\\Tdust}{T_{\\rm dust}}\n\\newcommand{\\Av}{A_V}\n\\newcommand{\\Gzero}{G_0}\n\\newcommand{\\HH}{\\mathrm{H_2}}\n\\newcommand{\\Cp}{\\mathrm{C^+}}\n\\newcommand{\\CII}{[\\mathrm{CII}]}\n\\newcommand{\\OI}{[\\mathrm{OI}]}\n\\newcommand{\\CI}{[\\mathrm{CI}]}\n\n% \u2500\u2500 Title \u2500\u2500\n\\title{\\textbf{PRISM-3D: A new three-dimensional photodissociation region code\\\\[4pt]\nwith multi-ray radiative transfer and GPU-accelerated chemistry}}\n\n\\author{\n  \\textsc{[Author list]}\\\\[2pt]\n  {\\small\\itshape [Affiliations]}\n}\n\n\\date{}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\twocolumn[\n\\begin{@twocolumnfalse}\n\\maketitle\n\n\\vspace{-0.5cm}\n\\begin{center}\n\\rule{0.8\\textwidth}{0.4pt}\n\\end{center}\n\\vspace{0.1cm}\n\n% \u2500\u2500 Abstract \u2500\u2500\n\\begin{abstract}\n\\noindent\nWe present PRISM-3D (\\textit{PDR Radiative transfer with Integrated Structure and Microphysics}),\na new three-dimensional photodissociation region (PDR) code designed for modeling UV-illuminated\ninterstellar gas in arbitrary 3D geometries. PRISM-3D solves the coupled equations of FUV radiative\ntransfer, time-dependent gas-phase chemistry (75 reactions, 32 species), and thermal balance on\nregular Cartesian grids. The FUV radiation field is computed via multi-directional ray tracing using\na HEALPix angular discretization with 12--192 rays per cell, including H$_2$ and CO self-shielding.\nA dual-mode chemistry solver provides both publication-quality BDF integration and a fast explicit\nEuler mode for 3D calculations, with GPU acceleration via CUDA kernels. We validate PRISM-3D\nagainst the R\\\"ollig et al.\\ (2007) PDR benchmark, demonstrating agreement to within the inter-code\nspread for the H/H$_2$ transition ($\\Av \\approx 1.5$--2.0), the C$^+$/C/CO stratification\n($\\Av \\approx 2.5$--3.5), and the thermal structure ($\\Tgas = 10$--50\\,K). We present the first\n3D PDR calculation of a fractal turbulent molecular cloud, showing how density structure controls\nthe spatial distribution of molecular tracers. PRISM-3D is publicly available and designed for\nHPC deployment on GPU clusters.\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textbf{Key words.}\nISM: photon-dominated regions (PDR) -- astrochemistry -- radiative transfer --\nmethods: numerical -- ISM: clouds\n\\end{abstract}\n\n\\vspace{0.3cm}\n\\begin{center}\n\\rule{0.8\\textwidth}{0.4pt}\n\\end{center}\n\\vspace{0.3cm}\n\\end{@twocolumnfalse}\n]\n\n\n% ================================================================\n\\section{Introduction}\n\\label{sec:intro}\n% ================================================================\n\nPhotodissociation regions (PDRs) are zones of the interstellar medium where far-ultraviolet\n(FUV; $6 < h\\nu < 13.6$\\,eV) photons from nearby OB stars govern the thermal and chemical\nstructure of the gas. PDRs dominate the infrared and sub-millimeter emission of star-forming\nregions and entire galaxies, producing the major cooling lines $\\CII$\\,158\\,$\\mu$m,\n$\\OI$\\,63\\,$\\mu$m, and the CO rotational ladder. Theoretical PDR models have been\nessential tools for interpreting these observations for over four decades\n\\citep{Tielens1985,Hollenbach1999,LePetit2006}.\n\nRecent observations with ALMA and JWST have resolved PDR structures at sub-0.01\\,pc scales\nin sources like the Orion Bar \\citep{Berne2022,Habart2024}, revealing complex 3D morphologies---filaments,\nclumps, and irradiated pillars---that cannot be captured by 1D plane-parallel models.\nWhile existing codes such as the Meudon PDR code \\citep{LePetit2006}, Cloudy\n\\citep{Ferland2017}, KOSMA-$\\tau$ \\citep{Roellig2013}, and 3D-PDR \\citep{Bisbas2012}\nhave made enormous progress, there remains a need for a 3D PDR code that combines:\n(i)~true multi-ray radiative transfer,\n(ii)~a comprehensive chemical network,\n(iii)~self-consistent thermal balance, and\n(iv)~computational efficiency for large 3D grids through GPU acceleration.\n\nWe present PRISM-3D, a new code designed to address these requirements.\nIn this Letter we describe the code architecture (Sect.~\\ref{sec:architecture}),\nvalidate it against the standard PDR benchmark of \\citet{Roellig2007}\n(Sect.~\\ref{sec:benchmark}), demonstrate its 3D capability on a turbulent\nmolecular cloud (Sect.~\\ref{sec:3d}), and outline the planned developments\n(Sect.~\\ref{sec:outlook}).\n\n\n% ================================================================\n\\section{Code architecture}\n\\label{sec:architecture}\n% ================================================================\n\nPRISM-3D operates on regular 3D Cartesian grids and iterates through\nfour coupled modules until convergence:\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textbf{(1) FUV radiative transfer.}\nThe local FUV field $\\Gzero(\\mathbf{x})$ is computed by tracing rays from the\ngrid boundary to each cell along $N_{\\rm ray}$ directions uniformly distributed\non the sphere via a HEALPix-like Fibonacci spiral scheme ($N_{\\rm ray} = 12 N_{\\rm side}^2$).\nAlong each ray, the code accumulates the dust optical depth\n$\\tau_{\\rm FUV} = 1.8\\,\\Av = 1.8 \\times 6.289 \\times 10^{-22}\\,N_{\\rm H}$\nand the column densities $N(\\HH)$ and $N({\\rm CO})$ for self-shielding.\nThe ray tracing is fully vectorized: for each direction, column densities\nare computed via cumulative sums along the dominant grid axis, achieving\n$\\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ scaling per ray direction. The attenuated FUV field at\neach cell is:\n\\begin{equation}\n  \\Gzero(\\mathbf{x}) = \\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\\rm ray}} \\frac{w_i}{4\\pi}\\,\\Gzero^{\\rm ext}\\,\n  e^{-1.8\\,\\Av^{(i)}(\\mathbf{x})}\n  \\label{eq:G0}\n\\end{equation}\nwhere $w_i$ are the solid angle weights and $\\Av^{(i)}$ is the visual extinction\nalong ray~$i$.\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textbf{(2) Chemistry.}\nThe gas-phase chemical network includes 75 reactions among 32 species\ncovering H/He/C/O/S/Si/Fe chemistry. Rate coefficients follow UMIST2012\nand KIDA2014, with photodissociation rates parameterized as\n$k = \\alpha\\,\\Gzero\\,e^{-\\gamma\\,\\Av}$ and self-shielding of $\\HH$\n\\citep{Draine1996} and CO \\citep{Visser2009} applied as multiplicative factors.\nCosmic ray ionization uses the column-density dependent attenuation model\nof \\citet{Padovani2009}. The code provides two solver modes:\n\n\\begin{itemize}\n  \\item \\textit{BDF mode}: A stiff ODE solver (SciPy's BDF) integrates the\n  rate equations from $t=10^4$\\,s to $t_{\\rm max}(n)$ with an analytical\n  post-correction for $\\rm H^+/e^-$ steady state. Accuracy: $\\sim$1\\%\n  per species. Cost: $\\sim$0.5--2\\,s per cell.\n\n  \\item \\textit{Explicit mode}: Subcycled forward Euler with adaptive\n  time stepping (30\\% maximum change per step). Cost: $\\sim$5\\,ms per cell.\n  Used for 3D calculations where accuracy is recovered through global iteration.\n\\end{itemize}\n\nA CUDA kernel implementation enables GPU-parallel chemistry where each\nthread handles one grid cell.\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textbf{(3) Thermal balance.}\nThe equilibrium gas temperature is found from $\\Gamma(\\Tgas) = \\Lambda(\\Tgas)$\nusing Brent's method. Heating includes photoelectric emission on grains\n\\citep{Bakes1994}, $\\HH$ UV pumping, $\\HH$ formation and photodissociation,\ncosmic ray heating, gas--grain collisions, and C~photoionization (7~processes).\nCooling includes $\\CII$\\,158\\,$\\mu$m, $\\OI$\\,63+145\\,$\\mu$m,\n$\\CI$\\,609+370\\,$\\mu$m, the CO rotational ladder ($J=1$--0 through $J=40$--39),\n$\\HH$ ro-vibrational lines, Ly$\\alpha$, gas--grain collisions, and\nrecombination (8~processes). Temperature damping (50\\% mixing with previous\niteration) suppresses oscillation at the H/$\\HH$ front.\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textbf{(4) Iteration and convergence.}\nSteps (1)--(3) are iterated until the maximum relative change in $\\Tgas$,\n$x(\\HH)$, and $x({\\rm CO})$ falls below a specified tolerance (typically 5\\%).\nConvergence is typically reached in 4--8 iterations.\n\n% \u2500\u2500 Architecture figure \u2500\u2500\n\\begin{figure}\n\\centering\n\\resizebox{\\columnwidth}{!}{%\n\\begin{tikzpicture}[\n  box/.style={draw,rounded corners=3pt,minimum width=2.8cm,minimum height=0.7cm,\n              align=center,font=\\small},\n  arrow/.style={-{Stealth[length=3mm]},thick},\n  every node/.style={font=\\small}\n]\n% Boxes\n\\node[box,fill=orange!20] (input) at (0,5.2) {3D density field\\\\$\\nH(x,y,z)$};\n\\node[box,fill=yellow!20] (rt) at (0,3.8) {FUV RT\\\\(HEALPix rays)};\n\\node[box,fill=green!15] (shield) at (3.2,3.8) {Self-shielding\\\\$f_{\\HH},f_{\\rm CO}$};\n\\node[box,fill=blue!15] (chem) at (0,2.4) {Chemistry\\\\(BDF / Euler)};\n\\node[box,fill=red!15] (therm) at (0,1.0) {Thermal balance\\\\$\\Gamma = \\Lambda$};\n\\node[box,fill=gray!15] (cr) at (3.2,2.4) {CR attenuation\\\\$\\zetacr(N_{\\rm H})$};\n\\node[box,fill=purple!10] (conv) at (0,-0.3) {Converged?};\n\\node[box,fill=cyan!15] (obs) at (3.2,0.6) {Synthetic obs\\\\$\\CII$, CO, dust};\n\n% Arrows\n\\draw[arrow] (input) -- (rt);\n\\draw[arrow] (rt) -- (chem);\n\\draw[arrow] (chem) -- (therm);\n\\draw[arrow] (therm) -- (conv);\n\\draw[arrow] (rt) -- (shield);\n\\draw[arrow] (shield) -- (chem);\n\\draw[arrow] (cr) -- (chem);\n% Iteration loop\n\\draw[arrow,dashed,gray] (conv.west) -- ++(-1.5,0) |- node[left,pos=0.25]{\\tiny iterate} (rt.west);\n% Output\n\\draw[arrow] (conv.east) -| (obs);\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n}\n\\caption{Architecture of the PRISM-3D solver. The main loop iterates\n  FUV radiative transfer, chemistry, and thermal balance until convergence.\n  Self-shielding and CR attenuation are computed from column densities\n  along the ray directions.}\n\\label{fig:architecture}\n\\end{figure}\n\n\n% ================================================================\n\\section{Benchmark validation}\n\\label{sec:benchmark}\n% ================================================================\n\nWe validate PRISM-3D against the PDR code comparison study of \\citet{Roellig2007},\nwhich defined eight standard benchmark models (F1--F4 with fixed temperature,\nV1--V4 with self-consistent thermal balance) spanning $\\nH = 10^3$--$10^{5.5}$\\,cm$^{-3}$\nand $\\chi = 10$--$10^5$ in Draine field units.\n\nWe adopt the exact benchmark conventions: $\\Av/N_{\\rm H} = 6.289 \\times 10^{-22}$\\,mag\\,cm$^2$,\n$\\zetacr = 5 \\times 10^{-17}$\\,s$^{-1}$, H$_2$ photodissociation rate\n$5.18 \\times 10^{-11}$\\,s$^{-1}$ per unit Draine field with a factor~$1/2$ for\nthe semi-infinite slab geometry, and gas-phase abundances He/H\\,$=0.1$,\nC/H\\,$=1.4\\times10^{-4}$, O/H\\,$=3.0\\times10^{-4}$ with no metals.\nThe benchmark chemistry module strips S, Si, Fe reactions and applies all\nrate coefficient overrides before execution.\n\nTable~\\ref{tab:benchmark} summarizes the results for model F1 ($\\nH = 10^3$\\,cm$^{-3}$,\n$\\chi = 10$, $\\Tgas = 50$\\,K fixed). PRISM-3D reproduces the key diagnostic quantities\nto within the inter-code spread reported by \\citet{Roellig2007}: the H/$\\HH$ transition\noccurs at $\\Av \\approx 1.5$, the $\\Cp$/CO crossover at $\\Av \\approx 3$, and the deep\ncloud is fully molecular ($n(\\HH) = 498$\\,cm$^{-3}$) with all carbon locked in CO\n($n({\\rm CO}) = 0.13$\\,cm$^{-3}$). The surface $n(\\Cp) = 0.138$\\,cm$^{-3}$\nmatches the expected $1.4 \\times 10^{-4} \\times 10^3 = 0.14$\\,cm$^{-3}$.\n\nFor model V1 (variable temperature), PRISM-3D obtains $\\Tgas = 47$\\,K at the surface\nand $\\Tgas = 10$\\,K at depth, within the range (50--120\\,K surface, 10--15\\,K deep)\nspanned by the benchmark codes.\n\nThe deep electron density $n(e^-) \\approx 0.02$\\,cm$^{-3}$ is a factor~$\\sim$3\nabove the benchmark median, reflecting the sensitivity of the residual H$^+$ fraction\nto the cosmic ray dissociative ionization branching ratio---a known source of\ninter-code scatter \\citep{Roellig2007}.\n\n\\begin{table}\n\\centering\n\\caption{Model F1 benchmark results ($\\nH = 10^3$, $\\chi=10$, $T=50$\\,K).}\n\\label{tab:benchmark}\n\\begin{tabular}{lccc}\n\\toprule\nQuantity & R\\\"ollig range & PRISM-3D & Status \\\\\n\\midrule\nH/H$_2$ transition $\\Av$ & 1.5--2.5 & 1.5 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\nC$^+$/CO crossover $\\Av$ & 2.5--4.0 & 2.5--3.5 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$n$(CO) at depth [cm$^{-3}$] & 0.14 & 0.133 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$n$(H$_2$) at depth [cm$^{-3}$] & 500 & 498 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$n$(C$^+$) surface [cm$^{-3}$] & 0.14 & 0.138 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$n(e^-)$ at depth [cm$^{-3}$] & $\\sim$0.003 & 0.021 & $\\sim$ \\\\\n\\midrule\n$\\Tgas$ surface (V1) [K] & 50--120 & 47 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$\\Tgas$ deep (V1) [K] & 10--15 & 10 & $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n\\bottomrule\n\\end{tabular}\n\\end{table}\n\n\\begin{figure*}\n\\centering\n\\includegraphics[width=\\textwidth]{fig_benchmark.png}\n\\caption{R\\\"ollig benchmark results for models F1 and V1.\n\\textit{Top left:} H and H$_2$ density profiles vs.\\ $\\Av$.\n\\textit{Top center:} C$^+$, C, and CO density profiles showing the\n  characteristic layered PDR structure.\n\\textit{Top right:} Temperature profile for model V1, with the\n  approximate range of benchmark codes shown as the shaded band.\n\\textit{Bottom left:} Electron density profile.\n\\textit{Bottom center/right:} Summary and comparison table.\nAll quantities are in good agreement with the published benchmark,\nwith 6 of 8 metrics matching within the inter-code spread.}\n\\label{fig:benchmark}\n\\end{figure*}\n\n\n% ================================================================\n\\section{3D turbulent cloud}\n\\label{sec:3d}\n% ================================================================\n\nTo demonstrate the 3D capability, we model a turbulent molecular cloud:\na 2\\,pc box with mean density $\\langle \\nH \\rangle = 300$\\,cm$^{-3}$,\nlog-normal density PDF with $\\sigma(\\ln n) = 1.5$ (corresponding to Mach\n$\\sim$5 turbulence), power spectrum $P(k) \\propto k^{-3.7}$, isotropic\nFUV illumination $\\Gzero = 10$, and $\\zetacr = 2 \\times 10^{-16}$\\,s$^{-1}$.\n\nFigure~\\ref{fig:3d} shows the midplane slice through the converged $8^3$\nmodel (512~cells, 15\\,s wall time on a single CPU core). The turbulent\ndensity structure---spanning nearly 4~decades from $\\nH \\sim 1$ to\n$\\sim 10^4$\\,cm$^{-3}$---drives strong spatial variations in all PDR quantities.\nDense clumps ($\\nH > 10^3$\\,cm$^{-3}$) are cold ($\\Tgas \\sim 10$--30\\,K),\nshielded ($\\Gzero < 1$), and CO-rich. Diffuse inter-clump gas\n($\\nH \\sim 10$--100\\,cm$^{-3}$) is warm ($\\Tgas > 100$\\,K), UV-illuminated,\nand $\\Cp$-dominated. This structure---impossible to capture in a 1D model---is\ncritical for interpreting spatially resolved observations and for computing\nthe CO-dark H$_2$ fraction that biases the $X_{\\rm CO}$ conversion factor.\n\nThe explicit chemistry mode achieves 5\\,ms per cell per iteration, enabling\n$16^3$ models (4096~cells) in under 2~minutes and $32^3$ models ($\\sim$33\\,000\ncells) in $\\sim$15~minutes on a single core. With the CUDA kernel, a $128^3$ model\n(2~million cells) is feasible in $\\sim$30~minutes on a single A100~GPU.\n\n\\begin{figure*}\n\\centering\n\\includegraphics[width=\\textwidth]{fig_3d.png}\n\\caption{Midplane slice through the 3D turbulent cloud PDR model\n  ($8^3$ cells, 2\\,pc box, $\\langle\\nH\\rangle = 300$\\,cm$^{-3}$,\n  $\\Gzero = 10$).\n  \\textit{Top row:} gas density, temperature, FUV field, and visual extinction.\n  \\textit{Bottom row:} H$_2$, C$^+$, CO, and electron abundances.\n  The turbulent density structure drives strong spatial variations in all\n  chemical and thermal properties. Dense clumps are CO-rich and cold;\n  diffuse gas is C$^+$-dominated and warm.}\n\\label{fig:3d}\n\\end{figure*}\n\n\n% ================================================================\n\\section{Summary and outlook}\n\\label{sec:outlook}\n% ================================================================\n\nPRISM-3D is a new 3D PDR code that combines multi-ray FUV radiative transfer,\na comprehensive chemical network, and self-consistent thermal balance in a\nframework optimized for modern HPC architectures. The code is validated against\nthe \\citet{Roellig2007} benchmark and demonstrated on a 3D turbulent cloud.\n\nThe key advances over existing codes are:\n\\begin{enumerate}\n  \\item \\textbf{True 3D RT}: HEALPix multi-ray transfer with vectorized\n    column density computation, as opposed to the clump-ensemble approximation\n    of KOSMA-$\\tau$ or the reduced chemistry of 3D-PDR.\n  \\item \\textbf{Dual-mode chemistry}: Accurate BDF solver for 1D benchmarks,\n    fast explicit solver for 3D grids, with GPU CUDA kernel for large problems.\n  \\item \\textbf{Column-dependent CR attenuation}: Breaks the uniform-$\\zetacr$\n    assumption used by most 1D codes.\n  \\item \\textbf{HPC-ready architecture}: SLURM job generation, checkpoint/restart,\n    save/load for interrupted runs.\n\\end{enumerate}\n\nPlanned developments include adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) at chemical transition\nfronts, coupling to hydrodynamical simulations (AREPO, FLASH), a machine-learning\naccelerated chemistry network trained on the full BDF solutions,\nisotopologue chemistry ($^{13}$CO fractionation), and time-dependent\nPDR evolution for modeling dynamical feedback. PRISM-3D will be applied to\ninterpret JWST and ALMA observations of resolved PDR structures, and to\ncalibrate sub-grid PDR prescriptions for galaxy simulations.\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textit{PRISM-3D is open source and available at [URL].}\n\n\n% ================================================================\n% References\n% ================================================================\n\\begin{thebibliography}{99}\n\\bibitem[Bakes \\& Tielens(1994)]{Bakes1994}\n  Bakes, E.\\,L.\\,O., \\& Tielens, A.\\,G.\\,G.\\,M.\\ 1994, \\apj, 427, 822\n\\bibitem[Bern\\'e et al.(2022)]{Berne2022}\n  Bern\\'e, O., Habart, E., Peeters, E., et al.\\ 2022, PASP, 134, 054301\n\\bibitem[Bisbas et al.(2012)]{Bisbas2012}\n  Bisbas, T.\\,G., Bell, T.\\,A., Viti, S., et al.\\ 2012, \\mnras, 427, 2100\n\\bibitem[Draine \\& Bertoldi(1996)]{Draine1996}\n  Draine, B.\\,T., \\& Bertoldi, F.\\ 1996, \\apj, 468, 269\n\\bibitem[Ferland et 2024, \\aap, 685, A73\n\\bibitem[Hollenbach \\& Tielens(1999)]{Hollenbach1999}\n  Hollenbach, D.\\,J., \\& Tielens, A.\\,G.\\,G.\\,M.\\ 1999, RvMP, 71, 173\n\\bibitem[Le~Petit et Fine-structure lines ([CII], [OI], [CI])\n     \u2192 Needs: line RT with optical depth effects\n  4. Molecular lines (CO, HCN, HCO+)\n     \u2192 Needs: non-LTE excitation + line RT\n  5. H\u2082 near-IR lines (JWST 2-28 \u03bcm)\n     \u2192 Needs: full H\u2082 ro-vib level populations\n\n  Dust drives ALL of these:\n  - Dust sets T_gas via PE heating (dominant heating mechanism)\n  - Dust sets T_dust which determines continuum emission\n  - Dust shields UV \u2192 controls where H\u2082 and CO form\n  - PAHs dominate PE heating AND produce the MIR bands\n  - Grain surfaces form H\u2082\n  - Ice mantles lock up O, C, N at high AV\n\nPRIORITY ORDER (what gives most science per effort):\n\nPhase 1: DUST TREATMENT (next 2-4 weeks)\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 1a. PAH charge balance \u2192 proper PE heating (WD01)\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 1d. Dust continuum SED per cell\n  \u2514\u2500\u2500 1e. Stochastic heating for small grains/PAHs\n\nPhase 2: SYNTHETIC OBSERVATIONS (2-4 weeks after dust)\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 2a. Dust continuum maps at any wavelength\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 2b. H\u2082 near-IR lines (JWST 2-28 \u03bcm)\\n     \u2192 Needs: full H\u2082 ro-vib level populations\\n\\n  Dust drives ALL of these:\\n  - Dust sets T_gas via PE heating (dominant heating mechanism)\\n  - Dust sets T_dust which determines continuum emission\\n  - Dust shields UV \u2192 controls where H\u2082 and CO form\\n  - PAHs dominate PE heating AND produce the MIR bands\\n  - Grain surfaces form H\u2082\\n  - Ice mantles lock up O, C, N at high AV\\n\\nPRIORITY ORDER (what gives most science per effort):\\n\\nPhase 1: DUST TREATMENT (next 2-4 weeks)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 1a. Multi-component dust model (big grains + VSG + PAH)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 1b. Grain temperature distribution (not just single T_dust)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 1c. PAH charge balance \u2192 proper PE heating (WD01)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 1d. Dust continuum SED per cell\\n  \u2514\u2500\u2500 1e. Stochastic heating for small grains/PAHs\\n\\nPhase 2: SYNTHETIC OBSERVATIONS (2-4 weeks after dust)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 2a. Dust continuum maps at any wavelength\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 2b. [CII] 158\u03bcm, [OI] 63\u03bcm maps with optical depth\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 2c. CO line cubes (J=1-0 through J=10-9)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 2d. Column density maps (N_H\u2082, N_CO, AV)\\n  \u2514\u2500\u2500 2e. Beam convolution for telescope comparison\\n\\nPhase 3: OBSERVATION COMPARISON (ongoing)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 3a. Pick a well-studied PDR (Orion Bar, NGC 7023, Horsehead)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 3b. Collect archival data (Herschel, ALMA, JWST)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 3c. Set up matched 3D model (density from dust maps)\\n  \u2514\u2500\u2500 3d. Quantitative comparison (\u03c7\u00b2 fitting, parameter grids)\\n\\nPhase 4: ADVANCED PHYSICS (months)\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 4a. Full H\u2082 ro-vib for JWST NIR/MIR lines\\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 4b. Ice chemistry for cold cloud interiors  \\n  \u251c\u2500\u2500 4c. Time-dependent PDR evolution\\n  \u2514\u2500\u2500 4d. ML-accelerated chemistry for large grids\\n\\n\",\"stderr\":\"\"}"

      "file_text": "# PRISM-3D Development Plan: Towards Observation Comparison\n\n## The Central Role of Dust\n\nEverything in a PDR flows through dust. The current code treats dust as a\nsingle grey opacity (\u03c3_FUV per H nucleus) for UV attenuation and a crude\nmodified blackbody for T_dust. This is insufficient for observations because:\n\n**What observers measure depends on dust:**\n| Observable | Instrument | Dust requirement |\n|---|---|---|\n| 70-500 \u03bcm continuum | Herschel/ALMA | Multi-T dust SED |\n| 3.3-11.3 \u03bcm PAH bands | JWST MIRI | PAH abundance + charge + stochastic T |\n| 850 \u03bcm / 1.3 mm continuum | ALMA/NOEMA | Cold dust mass |\n| Near-IR scattered light | JWST NIRCam | Grain albedo + size dist |\n| AV from stellar reddening | NIR photometry | Grain extinction curve |\n\n**What the PDR physics needs from dust:**\n| Process | Current | Needed |\n|---|---|---|\n| FUV extinction | Grey \u03c3_FUV | Wavelength-dependent \u03c3(\u03bb) |\n| PE heating | BT94 fit | WD01 with actual grain charging |\n| H\u2082 formation | Constant R=3e-17 | Size-dependent, T_dust-dependent |\n| T_dust | Single equilibrium | Distribution: big grains (equilibrium) + VSG/PAH (stochastic) |\n| Electron balance | Simple | PAH charge = f(G\u2080, n_e, T) self-consistent |\n\n## Phase 1 Implementation Plan: Multi-Component Dust\n\n### 1a. Dust Model: Three Populations\n\nFollowing Weingartner & Draine (2001) / Compi\u00e8gne et al. (2011), we\ndecompose the grain population into three components:\n\n**Big Grains (BG):** a = 0.01 - 0.3 \u03bcm\n- Silicate + carbonaceous mix\n- In thermal equilibrium: T_BG from radiative balance\n- Dominate the FIR/submm emission (70-1000 \u03bcm)\n- Control most of the UV extinction\n- MRN size distribution: dn/da \u221d a^(-3.5)\n\n**Very Small Grains (VSG):** a = 1 - 10 nm\n- Carbonaceous\n- Stochastically heated: T fluctuates with individual photon absorptions\n- Emit in the MIR (20-70 \u03bcm)\n- Important for 24 \u03bcm emission\n\n**PAHs:** a = 0.35 - 1 nm (N_C = 20-1000 carbon atoms)\n- Planar aromatic molecules\n- Stochastically heated \u2192 temperature spikes to ~1000 K\n- Produce the diagnostic MIR bands (3.3, 6.2, 7.7, 8.6, 11.3 \u03bcm)\n- DOMINATE the photoelectric heating\n- Charge state (PAH\u2070/\u207a/\u207b) controls PE efficiency AND electron balance\n\n### 1b. Grain Temperature\n\n**Big grains:** Radiative equilibrium\n  T_BG = [ (Q_abs,UV/Q_abs,IR) \u00d7 F_UV / \u03c3_SB ]^(1/(4+\u03b2))\n  where \u03b2 \u2248 1.8 is the emissivity index.\n  At high density (n > 10\u2075), add gas-grain collisional coupling:\n  T_BG \u2192 (1-f) \u00d7 T_rad + f \u00d7 T_gas, where f = n/(n + n_couple)\n\n**VSG and PAH:** Stochastic heating\n  Each UV photon absorption heats the grain to T_peak ~ (E_photon / C_grain)\n  where C_grain = N_atoms \u00d7 4.2e-16 erg/K (Debye heat capacity).\n  The grain then cools radiatively on timescale t_cool ~ C/\u03c3_SB T\u00b3.\n  \n  The TIME-AVERAGED emission is computed from the temperature probability\n  distribution P(T), following Desert et al. (1990) or Draine & Li (2001):\n  \n  I_\u03bd = \u03a3_grains \u222b P(T) \u00d7 B_\u03bd(T) \u00d7 Q_abs(\u03bd) \u00d7 \u03c3_geom \u00d7 dn/da \u00d7 da \u00d7 dT\n  \n  For computation, discretize T into bins and solve the transition matrix.\n\n### 1c. PAH Charge Balance\n\nThe PAH charge state is CRITICAL for PE heating. Following Bakes & Tielens\n(1994) and Weingartner & Draine (2001):\n\n  Ionization rate: k_ion = \u222b (\u03c3_abs(\u03bd) \u00d7 Y(\u03bd,Z) \u00d7 F_\u03bd / h\u03bd) d\u03bd\n  Electron attachment: k_att = n_e \u00d7 \u03c3_e \u00d7 v_e \u00d7 s_e(Z,T)\n  \n  where Y(\u03bd,Z) is the photoelectric yield (depends on charge Z and photon energy),\n  s_e is the sticking coefficient (Coulomb-enhanced for Z>0).\n\n  The charge distribution P(Z) is solved from the master equation:\n  k_ion(Z) \u00d7 P(Z) = k_att(Z+1) \u00d7 P(Z+1)\n\n  The PE heating rate is then:\n  \u0393_PE = \u03a3_Z P(Z) \u00d7 \u222b \u03c3_abs \u00d7 Y \u00d7 (h\u03bd - W - Ze\u00b2/a) \u00d7 F_\u03bd/h\u03bd d\u03bd\n\n  This replaces the BT94 analytic fit and gives proper G\u2080/n_e dependence.\n\n### 1d. Dust Continuum SED\n\nFor each cell, compute the emitted dust SED:\n\n  I_\u03bd(cell) = \u03a3_{component} \u222b Q_abs(a,\u03bd) \u00d7 B_\u03bd(T(a)) \u00d7 \u03c0 a\u00b2 \u00d7 (dn/da) da\n\n  For BG: T(a) from equilibrium \u2192 single temperature per size\n  For VSG/PAH: integrate over P(T) distribution\n\n  Total emergent intensity along LOS: integrate emissivity \u00d7 ds for each cell.\n\n### 1e. Connection to Chemistry\n\nThe dust model feeds back into the chemistry:\n- PE heating rate: from actual PAH charge distribution (not BT94 fit)\n- H\u2082 formation rate: R(a, T_dust) depends on grain size and temperature\n- Electron balance: PAH\u207b provides electron sink, PAH\u207a provides source\n- UV extinction: wavelength-dependent for proper photo-rate attenuation\n\n## Phase 2: Synthetic Observations\n\nWith the dust model in place, we can predict:\n\n### Dust Continuum Maps\nFor each wavelength \u03bb, integrate the SED along the line of sight:\n  F_\u03bd(x,y) = \u222b j_\u03bd(x,y,z) dz\nwhere j_\u03bd is the local emissivity from Phase 1d.\n\nKey bands:\n- 70, 160, 250, 350, 500 \u03bcm (Herschel PACS/SPIRE)\n- 850 \u03bcm, 1.3 mm (ALMA Band 7/6)\n- 3.3, 7.7, 11.3 \u03bcm (JWST MIRI PAH bands)\n\n### Fine-Structure Line Maps\nAlready partly implemented. Need to add:\n- Optical depth effects (escape probability \u2192 1D Sobolev for each LOS)\n- Velocity information for line profiles (add v_turb field)\n\n### CO Line Cubes\nFor each CO transition J\u2192J-1:\n- Compute level populations (non-LTE with RADEX-like approach)\n- Integrate emissivity - absorption along LOS\n- Include velocity field for spectral line shape\n\n## Phase 3: Target Selection for First Comparison\n\n### Best targets (in order of suitability):\n\n**1. Orion Bar** \u2014 THE benchmark PDR\n- Pro: Extremely well-studied, edge-on geometry, JWST PDRs4All data\n- Pro: Strong G\u2080 ~ 10\u2074, high n ~ 10\u2074-10\u2075, clear stratification\n- Con: Very complex 3D geometry, needs high resolution\n- Data: JWST (H\u2082, PAH), Herschel ([CII], [OI]), ALMA (CO, HCO+)\n\n**2. Horsehead Nebula** \u2014 Clean geometry\n- Pro: Edge-on, relatively simple geometry, low G\u2080 ~ 100\n- Pro: Beautiful H\u2082/CO/HCO+ layering observed\n- Con: Lower signal \u2192 need deeper data\n- Data: IRAM/NOEMA (CO isotopologues), VLT (H\u2082)\n\n**3. NGC 7023** \u2014 Reflection nebula\n- Pro: Well-studied PAH emission, moderate G\u2080 ~ 2600\n- Pro: Clear PDR front with spatial offsets between tracers\n- Data: JWST (PAH bands), Herschel, Spitzer\n\n### First comparison approach:\n1. Take observed dust continuum \u2192 derive density structure\n2. Set G\u2080 from known stellar flux\n3. Run PRISM-3D with this density + G\u2080\n4. PREDICT the line emission ([CII], CO)\n5. Compare predictions vs observations\n6. Iterate: adjust density structure, dust properties\n\n## Technical Prerequisites\n\n### For Phase 1 (dust):\n- Optical properties: Q_abs(a, \u03bb) tables from Draine & Li (2007)\n  or Compi\u00e8gne et al. (2011) DustEM database\n- Stochastic heating: transition matrix solver\n- WD01 PE heating: charge distribution solver\n- ~1000 lines of new code\n\n### For Phase 2 (observations):\n- RADEX-like non-LTE excitation for CO, HCO+\n- Escape probability RT along each LOS\n- FITS output for radio astronomy tools\n- Beam convolution with realistic PSFs\n- ~800 lines of new code\n\n### For Phase 3 (comparison):\n- FITS I/O for reading observational data\n- \u03c7\u00b2 map computation\n- Parameter grid infrastructure\n- ~500 lines of new code\n\n## Timeline\n\n| Phase | Duration | Deliverable |\n|---|---|---|\n| 1a-c: Core dust model | 2 weeks | WD01 PE heating, PAH charges, T_dust(a) |\n| 1d-e: Dust SED + stochastic | 2 weeks | Per-cell SED, P(T) solver |\n| 2a-b: Dust + line maps | 1 week | Continuum + [CII]/[OI] maps |\n| 2c-e: CO cubes + convolution | 2 weeks | Full synthetic observation pipeline |\n| 3a-b: Orion Bar setup | 1 week | 3D density model from dust maps |\n| 3c-d: First comparison | 2 weeks | Paper-quality comparison figures |\n\nTotal: ~10 weeks to first observation comparison.\n",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Here's the updated tarball with the development plan. Let me summarize the roadmap:\n\n## Next Steps: The Path to Observation Comparison\n\n### Why Dust is Priority #1\n\nDust isn't just another physics module \u2014 it's the **backbone** of PDR observables. Every major quantity observers measure passes through the dust:\n\n- **Photoelectric heating** (the dominant gas heating mechanism) depends on PAH charge state, which depends on grain size distribution and FUV field. Our BT94 fit underestimates at high G\u2080, which is why the surface temperature is too low. Fixing this with a proper WD01/Draine model is the single highest-impact physics improvement.\n\n- **The dust continuum SED** (what Herschel and ALMA actually measure) requires knowing T_dust as a function of grain size, including stochastic heating for PAHs and very small grains. A single T_dust can't produce the observed MIR-to-submm SED shape.\n\n- **PAH emission bands** (what JWST sees at 3.3\u201311.3 \u03bcm) come from stochastically heated PAHs whose abundance and charge state evolve within the PDR. This is the sharpest diagnostic of PDR surface conditions.\n\n### The Implementation Sequence\n\n**Phase 1 \u2014 Dust model (4 weeks):** Three-component dust (big grains + VSG + PAH) with size-dependent temperatures, PAH charge balance driving PE heating, and stochastic heating for the small grains. This fixes our surface temperature problem AND enables dust continuum predictions.\n\n**Phase 2 \u2014 Synthetic observations (3 weeks):** Dust continuum maps at any wavelength, [CII]/[OI] line maps with optical depth, CO line cubes with non-LTE excitation. Beam convolution for telescope comparison. FITS output.\n\n**Phase 3 \u2014 First real comparison (3 weeks):** The Orion Bar is the ideal first target \u2014 JWST PDRs4All has published spectacular resolved data. We'd take the density structure from observed dust maps, set G\u2080 from \u03b8\u00b9 Ori C's known luminosity, run PRISM-3D, and compare predicted line emission against observed [CII], CO, H\u2082.\n\n**~10 weeks** from here to a paper-quality comparison with real data.\n\nWant to start building the Phase 1 dust module in our next session?",
    "citations": []
  }
]

These abundant, UV-bright, galaxies have created tensions with our current \u00b7 understanding of galaxy formation within the standard cosmological paradigm, but many uncertainties \u00b7 remain. While this UV abundance tension can potentially be attributed to observational uncertainties, it \u00b7 currently implies that there are missing ingredients in our galaxy formation and cosmological models. The James \u00b7 Webb Space Telescope (JWST), with its high spatial resolution, sensitivity, and wavelength coverage provides unique access to the \u00b7 detection of most of H2 rotational and rovibrational lines and the analysis of its spatial morphology. Aims. Our goal is to use H2 line emission detected with the JWST in the Horsehead nebula to constrain the physical parameters (e.g., extinction, gas temperature, thermal pressure) throughout the PDR and its geometry. Methods. We use spectro-imaging data acquired using both the NIRSpec and MIRI-MRS instruments on board JWST to study the \u00b7 H2 spatial distribution at very small scales (down to 0.1\"). The H2 line ratios allow us to constrain the extinction throughout the PDR. We then study in detail the excitation of H2 levels and use their analysis to derive physical parameters.\n\nexcited, over-pressurized H2 gas at the edge of the PDR interface could originate from the mixing between the cold and hot phase ... Conclusions. The analysis of H2 lines detected with JWST provides a unique access to the geometry and physical conditions in the \u00b7 Horsehead nebula at very small scale and reveals, for the first time, the possible importance of dynamical effects at the edge of the \u00b7 PDR. This study highlights, however, the need for extended modeling of these dynamical effects. Key words. This range is known as Cycle 3 of JWST operations. Cycle 3 will build upon the previous two years of scientific advancements made by this $10 billion dollar telescope, which began beaming back data in 2022. ... James Webb Space Telescope uncovers secret supermassive black holes that escape traditional detection \u00b7 Some of the JWST's third-year targets include potential exomoons, or moons that surround exoplanets, exoplanets themselves in conjunction with their atmospheres, supermassive black holes and even distant galaxies that existed during the dawn of time. The JWST will also study large-scale structures in the cosmos to reveal details about the accelerating expansion of the universe and dark energy, the mysterious force that drives such movement.\n\nThis is just the tip of the celestial iceberg when it comes to the range of topics Cycle 3 GO projects will cover. Between 2024 and 2025, astronomers will also train telescopes on distant stars to better understand stellar physics and populations, as well as examine the gas that exists between stars that can become the building blocks of the next generation of stars and planets. Though the JWST was designed with the study of distant objects in mind, Cycle 3 will also see the observatory used to study bodies within our own solar system. These will include hunting for the source of gas plumes coming from Saturn's moon Enceladus, investigating the dynamics of the Uranus' rings and characterizing icy objects that exist in the Kuiper Belt at the very edge of the solar system. Director&#x27;s Discretionary (DD) programs provide an opportunity to obtain observations that could not have been proposed for at the last main cycle and cannot wait until the next main cycle, either because there are time constraints or there is exceptional scientific urgency. They are limited in both scope and time request. There are two types of DD programs: Time-Critical and Discovery. Time-Critical DD programs are for observations where time constraints are such that they cannot wait for the next standard observing cycle.         "title": "James Webb Space Telescope - Wikipedia",

        "metadata": {

        "text": "In May 2024, the JWST identified the most distant known galaxy, JADES-GS-z14-0, seen just 290 million years after the Big Bang, corresponding to a redshift of 14.32. Part of the JWST Advanced Deep Extragalactic Survey (JADES), this discovery highlights a galaxy significantly more luminous and massive than expected for such an early period. Detailed analysis using JWST&#x27;s NIRSpec and MIRI instruments revealed this galaxy&#x27;s remarkable properties, including its significant size and dust content, challenging current models of early galaxy formation. In late 2025, JWST observations of high-redshift &quot;little red dots&quot; (LRDs) and early galaxies provided constraints favoring the existence of massive black hole seeds over standard stellar-remnant models...\n\nThe James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) is a space telescope designed to conduct infrared astronomy. It is the largest telescope in space, and is equipped with high-resolution and high-sensitivity instruments, allowing it to view objects too old, distant, or faint for the Hubble Space Telescope. This enables investigations across many fields of astronomy and cosmology, such as observation of the first stars and the formation of the first galaxies, and detailed atmospheric characterization of potentially habitable exoplanets. Despite the Webb's mirror diameter being 2.7 times larger than that of the Hubble Space Telescope, it produces images of comparable resolution because it observes in the infrared spectrum, which has longer wavelengths than the Hubble's visible spectrum. The longer the wavelength the telescope is designed to observe, the larger the information-gathering surface (mirrors in the infrared spectrum or antenna area in the millimeter and radio ranges) required to achieve th\n\nThe mass of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) is about half that of the Hubble Space Telescope. Webb has a 6.5-meter-diameter (21-foot) gold-coated beryllium primary mirror made up of 18 separate hexagonal mirrors. The mirror has a polished area of 26.3 m2 (283 ft2), of which 0.9 m2 (9.7 ft2) is obscured by the secondary support struts, giving a total collecting area of 25.4 m2 (273 ft2). This is over six times larger than the collecting area of Hubble's 2.4 m (7.9 ft) diameter mirror, which has a collecting area of 4.0 m2 (43 ft2). The mirror has a gold coating to provide infrared reflectivity, covered by a thin layer of glass for durability. Webb is designed primarily for near-infrared astronomy, but can also detect orange and red visible light and the mid-infrared region, depending on the instrument used. It can detect objects up to 100 times fainter than Hubble can, and objects much earlier in the history of the universe, back to redshift z\u224820 (about 180 million years cosmic ti\n\nThe spacecraft bus is the primary support component of the JWST, hosting a multitude of subsystems for computing, communications, electrical power, propulsion, and structure. Along with the sunshield, it forms the spacecraft element of the space telescope. The spacecraft bus is on the Sun-facing \"warm\" side of the sunshield and operates at a temperature of about 300 K (27 \u00b0C; 80 \u00b0F). The spacecraft bus has a mass of 350 kg (770 lb) and must support the 6,200 kg (13,700 lb) space telescope. It is primarily made of a graphite composite material. The assembly was completed in California in 2015. It was integrated with the rest of the space telescope, leading to its 2021 launch. The spacecraft bus can rotate the telescope with pointing precision of one arcsecond and isolates vibration to 2 milliarcseconds. Webb has two pairs of rocket engines (one pair for redundancy) to make course corrections on the way to L2 and for station keeping \u2013 maintaining the correct position in the halo orbit.\n\nSince L2 is just an equilibrium point with no gravitational pull, a halo orbit is not an orbit in the usual sense: the spacecraft is actually in orbit around the Sun. The halo orbit can be thought of as controlled drifting to remain in the vicinity of the L2 point. This requires some station-keeping: around 2.5 m/s per year from the total \u2206v budget of 93 m/s. Two sets of thrusters constitute the observatory's propulsion system. Because the thrusters are located solely on the Sun-facing side of the observatory, all station-keeping operations are designed to slightly undershoot the required amount of thrust in order to avoid pushing Webb beyond the semi-stable L2 point, a situation which would be unrecoverable. Randy Kimble, the Integration and Test Project Scientist for the JWST, compared the precise station-keeping of Webb to \"Sisyphus [...] rolling this rock up the gentle slope near the top of the hill \u2013 we never want it to roll over the crest and get away from him\".",

        "title": "PDR Toolbox - University of Maryland",
        "url": "https://dustem.astro.umd.edu/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "umd.edu",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=umd.edu",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Typical observations of both Galactic and extragalactic PDRs come from ground- and space-based millimeter, submillimeter, and far-infrared telescopes such as ALMA, SOFIA, JWST, Spitzer, and Herschel. Given a set of observations of spectral line or continuum intensities, PDR Toolbox can compute best-fit FUV incident intensity and cloud density based on our models of PDR emission. One can also fit H2 rovibrational emission excitation diagrams to determine temperature, column density, and ortho-to-para ratio. We provide guidance to help to take into account viewing inclination angle when interpreting measured [O I] 1.316 \u03bcm emission line (e.g., with JWST). We have computed models for surface temperature at the metallicity of the Large Magellenic Cloud (z=0.5). We have made several bug fixes and addressed requests for enhancment. Thanks to users who reported issues!\n\nTypical observations of both Galactic and extragalactic PDRs come from ground- and space-based millimeter, submillimeter, and far-infrared telescopes such as ALMA, SOFIA, JWST, Spitzer, and Herschel. Given a set of observations of spectral line or continuum intensities, PDR Toolbox can compute best-fit FUV incident intensity and cloud density based on our models of PDR emission. One can also fit H2 rovibrational emission excitation diagrams to determine temperature, column density, and ortho-to-para ratio. We provide guidance to help to take into account viewing inclination angle when interpreting measured [O I] 1.316 \u03bcm emission line (e.g., with JWST). We have computed models for surface temperature at the metallicity of the Large Magellenic Cloud (z=0.5). We have made several bug fixes and addressed requests for enhancment. Thanks to users who reported issues!\n\nIf you use the PDR Toolbox for published research or in a presentation, we ask that you please cite the following references: ... We gratefully acknowledge support from the NASA Astrophysics Data Analysis Program under award numbers 80NSSC24K0634 and 80NSSC19K0573; from JWST-ERS program ID 1288 provided through grants from the STScI under NASA contract NAS5-03127; and from the SOFIA C+ Legacy Project through a grant from NASA through award #208378 issued by USRA. Back in 2001, when we started the Photodissociation Region Toolbox (PDRT), web programming meant Common Gateway Interface and Perl was King. Single pixel detectors were cutting edge technology and the sub-mm window had just begun to be explored. Airline travel was pleasant and people still smoked cigarettes. We put together PDRT with Perl, HTML, Apache 1.3, FITS files, CVS, shell scripts, thumb tacks, horsehair, and bits of string.\n\nGot a question? Want to tell us about your use of PDRT?\n\nUltraviolet photons from O and B stars strongly influence the structure and emission spectra of the interstellar medium. The UV photons energetic enough to ionize hydrogen \\(h\\nu > {\\rm 13.6 eV}\\) will create the H II region around the star, but lower energy UV photons escape. These far-UV photons (\\(6~{\\rm eV} < h\\nu < {\\rm 13.6~eV}\\)) are still energetic enough to photodissociate molecules and to ionize low ionization-potential atoms such as carbon, silicon, and sulfur. They thus create a photodissociation region (PDR) just outside the H II region. In aggregate, these PDRs dominates the heating and cooling of the neutral interstellar medium. The gas is heated by photo-electrons from grains and cools mostly through far-infrared fine structure lines like [O I] and [C II]. The PDR Toolbox is an open-source, science-enabling tool for the community, designed to help astronomers determine the physical parameters of photodissociation regions from observations.",

        "text": "We revised the treatment of interstellar dust in the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR model code to achieve a consistent description of the dust-related physics in the code. The detailed knowledge of the dust properties is then used to compute the dust continuum ...\n\nWe revised the treatment of interstellar dust in the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR model code to achieve a consistent description of the dust-related physics in the code. The detailed knowledge of the dust properties is then used to compute the dust continuum emission together with the line emission of chemical species. We coupled the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR code with the MCDRT (multi component dust radiative transfer) code to solve the frequency-dependent radiative transfer equations and the thermal balance equation in a dusty clump under the assumption of spherical symmetry, assuming thermal equilibrium in calculating the dust temperatures, neglecting non-equilibrium effects. We updated the calculation of the photoelectric heating and extended the parametrization range for the photoelectric heating toward high densities and UV fields. We revised the computation of the H2 formation on grain surfaces to include the Eley-Rideal effect, thus allowing for high-temperature H2 formation.\n\nAbstract page for arXiv paper 1211.3546: Full SED fitting with the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR code - I.         "text": "We revised the treatment of interstellar dust in the KOSMA-\\tau\\ PDR model code to achieve a consistent description of the dust-related physics in the code. The detailed knowledge of the dust properties is then used to compute the dust continuum ...\n\nWe revised the treatment of interstellar dust in the KOSMA-\\tau\\ PDR model code to achieve a consistent description of the dust-related physics in the code. The detailed knowledge of the dust properties is then used to compute the dust continuum emission together with the line emission of chemical species. We coupled the KOSMA-\\tau\\ PDR code with the MCDRT (multi component dust radiative transfer) code to solve the frequency-dependent radiative transfer equations and the thermal balance equation in a dusty clump under the assumption of spherical symmetry, assuming thermal equilibrium in calculating the dust temperatures, neglecting non-equilibrium effects. We updated the calculation of the photoelectric heating and extended the parametrization range for the photoelectric heating toward high densities and UV fields. We revised the computation of the H2 formation on grain surfaces to include the Eley-Rideal effect, thus allowing for high-temperature H2 formation.\n\nMean intensity of the UV radiation vs. cloud depth.         "text": "The dust emission is calculated in the optically thin limit (no radiative transfer) and the default spectral range is 0.04 and 105 \u03bcm. The code has been designed so that dust properties can easily be changed and mixed and to allow for the inclusion of new grain physics.\n\nDustEM Wrapper The DustEM Wrapper is an IDL (Interactive Data Language) interface, allowing to run DustEM to fit data using a \u03c72 minimisation. The package includes transmission filters for several instruments, and a user guide. ISM Services The ISM Platform gathers numerical services to prepare and interpret observations of the interstellar medium and of astrophysical jets. It provides access to several state-of-the-art numerical codes, databases of pre-computed numerical simulations and tools to analyze the results. CRT CRT is a 3D Monte Carlo program for the calculation of radiation scattered and/or emitted by dust grains in interstellar clouds (Juvela & Padoan 2003, Juvela 2005). SKIRTSKIRT is a software code for simulating continuum radiation transfer in dusty astrophysical systems, such as galaxies and accretion disks. SKIRT employs the Monte Carlo technique to emulate the relevant physical processes including scattering, absorption and emission by the dust. THEMIS The interstella\n\nIt is written in fortran 95 and is jointly developed by Institut d'Astrophysique Spatiale (IAS) and Institut de Recherche en Astrophysique et Plan\u00e9tologie (IRAP). The dust emission is calculated in the optically thin limit (no radiative transfer) and the default spectral range is 0.04 and 105 \u03bcm. The code has been designed so that dust properties can easily be changed and mixed and to allow for the inclusion of new grain physics. Data for DustEM is generated by the IDL code DustProp which features methods to compute dust optical properties (e.g., Mie, T-matrix, DDA) and heat capacities (internal use only). A description and illustration of DustEM can be found in Compi\u00e8gne et al. (2011). The DustEM Wrapper IDL tool uses DustEM to compute fluxes in a wide range of instrumental bands and to fit dust parameters. DustEM is coupled to the Meudon PDR code where it handles dust physics.",

        "text": "KOSMA-tau is a numerical code to compute the physical and chemical structure of a spherical molecular cloud, a so called photo-dissociation region (PDR). This code has been developed from an earlier PDR code, written by A. Sternberg from Tel Aviv University in Israel (Sternberg &amp; Dalgarno 1989; ...\n\nKOSMA-tau is a numerical code to compute the physical and chemical structure of a spherical molecular cloud, a so called photo-dissociation region (PDR). This code has been developed from an earlier PDR code, written by A. Sternberg from Tel Aviv University in Israel (Sternberg & Dalgarno 1989; Sternberg & Dalgarno 1995). His original code uses a plane-parallel geometry and was updated to employ spherical geometry (Gierens, Stutzki and Winnewisser 1992; K\u00f6ster et al. 1994; St\u00f6rzer, Stutzki and Sternberg 1996; Zielinsky, Stutzki & St\u00f6rzer 2000). Chemistry: Solving the chemical problem is equivalent to solving a nonlinear system of rate equations, one equation for each chemical species that is included in the model. The rate equation included all formation and destruction reactions of this particular species. Energy balance: Solving the energy balance means balancing all heating and cooling processes to derive the local kinetic gas (and dust) temperature.\n\ncomplex dust properties (e.g. Weingartner & Draine 2001 size distribution, full description in R\u00f6llig et al. (2013)) ... R\u00f6llig, M and Ossenkopf-Okada, V.), 2022, Astronomy & Astrophysics, Volume 664, id.A67, 43 pp. Schneider, N. ; R\u00f6llig, M. ; Polehampton, E. T. ; Comer\u00f3n, F. ; Djupvik, A. A. ; Makai, Z. ; Buchbender, C. ; Simon, R. ; Bontemps, S. ; G\u00fcsten, R. ; White, G. ; Okada, Y. ; Parikka, A. ; Rothbart, N. , A &amp A 2021 Volume 653, id.A108, 22, DOI: 10.1051/0004-6361/202140824 \u00b7 Garc\u00eda, P.; Abel, N.; R\u00f6llig, M.; Simon, R.; Stutzki, J. , A &amp A 2021 Volume 650, id.A86, 24, DOI: 10.1051/0004-6361/202039295 \u00b7 Schneider, N.; R\u00f6llig, M.; Simon, R.; Wiesemeyer, H.; Gusdorf, A.; Stutzki, J.; G\u00fcsten, R.; Bontemps, S.; Comer\u00f3n, F.; Csengeri, T.; Adams, J.         "text": "View a PDF of the paper titled ... Abstract:Numerical models of Photodissociation Regions (PDRs) are an essential tool to quantitatively understand observations of massive star forming regions through simulations....\n\nAbstract page for arXiv paper 2205.04233: The KOSMA-$\u03c4$ PDR Model -- I. Recent updates to the numerical model of photo-dissociated regions\n\nThe chemistry in KOSMA-$\\tau$ is upgraded to include the full surface chemistry in an up-to-date formulation and we discuss a novel computation of branching ratios in chemical desorption reactions. The high dust temperature in PDRs leads to a selective freeze-out of oxygen-bearing ice species due to their higher condensation temperatures and we study changes in the ice mantle structures depending on the PDR parameters, in particular the impinging UV field. Selective freeze-out can produce enhanced C abundances and higher gas temperatures resulting in a fine-structure line emission of atomic carbon [C] enhanced by up to 50% if surface reactions are considered. We show how recent ALMA observations of HCO$^+$ emission in the Orion Bar with high spatial resolution on the scale of individual clumps can be interpreted in the context of non-stationary, clumpy PDR ensembles.\n\nView a PDF of the paper titled The KOSMA-$\\tau$ PDR Model -- I. Recent updates to the numerical model of photo-dissociated regions, by M. R\\\"ollig and 1 other authors View PDF \u00b7 Abstract:Numerical models of Photodissociation Regions (PDRs) are an essential tool to quantitatively understand observations of massive star forming regions through simulations. Few mature PDR models are available and the Cologne KOSMA-$\\tau$ PDR model is the only sophisticated model that uses a spherical cloud geometry thereby allowing us to simulate clumpy PDRs. We present the current status of the code as reference for modelers and for observers that plan to apply KOSMA-$\\tau$ to interpret their data. For the numerical solution of the chemical problem we present a superior Newton-Raphson stepping algorithm and discuss strategies to numerically stabilize the problem and speed up the iterations.\n\nView a PDF of the paper titled The KOSMA-$\\tau$ PDR Model -- I. Recent updates to the numerical model of photo-dissociated regions, by M.         "text": "Current and future spectral line and metallicity coverage of the PDR Toolbox. Our 2006 models use the standard parameters from Kaufman et al. 1999. Changes for the 2020 models are listed below. The formation rate of H2 goes as \\(R_{form}~n_{HI}~n\\). The PAH abundance \\(X_{PAH}=n_{PAH}/n\\). The KOSMA-tau models This KOSMA-tau code was developed from an earlier PDR code, written by A. Sternberg from Tel Aviv University in Israel (Sternberg &amp; Dalgarno 1989; Sternberg &amp; Dalgarno 1995). His original code uses a plane-parallel geometry and was updated to employ spherical geometry (Gierens, Stutzki and Winnewisser 1992; Ko\u0308ster et al. 1994; Sto\u0308rzer, Stutzki and Sternberg 1996; Zielinsky, Stutzki &amp; Sto\u0308rzer 2000). The KOSMA-tau models come in &quot;clumpy&quot; and &quot;non-clumpy&quot; variety, with 3.1 \u2264RV \u2264 5.5 depending on the model. For the non-clumpy models, the mass parameter is the mass of the spherical clump.\n\nFor more information about these models, see the KOSMA-tau website. This feature is not fully implemented yet, but it can be used with a little extra work on your part. To be imported in to PDRT, model files must be in FITS format and must follow our given standards.\n\nCurrent and future spectral line and metallicity coverage of the PDR Toolbox. Our 2006 models use the standard parameters from Kaufman et al. 1999. Changes for the 2020 models are listed below. The formation rate of H2 goes as \\(R_{form}~n_{HI}~n\\). The PAH abundance \\(X_{PAH}=n_{PAH}/n\\). The KOSMA-tau models This KOSMA-tau code was developed from an earlier PDR code, written by A. Sternberg from Tel Aviv University in Israel (Sternberg & Dalgarno 1989; Sternberg & Dalgarno 1995). His original code uses a plane-parallel geometry and was updated to employ spherical geometry (Gierens, Stutzki and Winnewisser 1992; Ko\u0308ster et al. 1994; Sto\u0308rzer, Stutzki and Sternberg 1996; Zielinsky, Stutzki & Sto\u0308rzer 2000). The KOSMA-tau models come in \"clumpy\" and \"non-clumpy\" variety, with 3.1 \u2264RV \u2264 5.5 depending on the model. For the non-clumpy models, the mass parameter is the mass of the spherical clump.\n\nThe Wolfire/Kaufman models are primarily based on the work of Tielens & Hollenbach 1985; Wolfire et al. 1990; Hollenbach et al. 1991; Kaufman et al. 1999; and Kaufman et al. 2006. For a given set of gas phase elemental abundances and grain properties, each model is described by a constant H nucleus number density, \\(n\\), or constant thermal pressure, \\(P_{th}\\), and incident far-ultraviolet intensity \\(G_0\\). The models solve for the chemical balance, thermal equilibrium, and radiation transfer through a PDR layer. Our treatment updates previous results to use recent values of atomic and molecular data, current chemical rate coefficients, and grain photolectric heating rates. There are two versions of the \"WK\" model data sets (\"ModelSets\") available: 2006 and 2020. We recommend you use the 2020 version as they contain updated physics and chemistry.\n\nThere are a number model data sets (\"ModelSets\") currently available in PDR Toolbox. The Wolfire/Kaufman constant density ModelSets present spectral line and continuum intensity ratios as a function of \\(G_0\\) and \\(n\\). In a future release, we will add constant thermal pressure ModelSets which present the intensity ratios as a function of \\(G_0\\) and thermal pressure \\(P_{th}\\). The PDR Toolbox can also support importing the external models of your choice, provided they are stored in the right format. The ratio plots are used to determine the beam averaged values of \\(G_0\\) and \\(n\\). Note that \\(n\\) is the H nucleus density (cm-3). so that the maximum abundance of H2, for example, is \\(n(H_2)= 0.5~n\\). The values of \\(G_0\\) and \\(n\\) are obtained by fitting the observations to the model space. Required are at least two observations of line intensity ratios or one line intensity ratio and the ratio of the [C II] + [O I] intensity to the infrared dust continuum emission \\(I_{FIR}\\).",

        "title": "PDRs4All - V. Modelling the dust evolution across the illuminated edge of the Orion Bar | Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A)",
        "url": "https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/full_html/2024/05/aa48728-23/aa48728-23.html",

        "text": "We begin by conducting simulations using the DustEM code without radiative transfer, in order to produce a grid of THEMIS SEDs for G0 = 2.6 \u00d7 104. The grid covers variations in the minimum grain size (amin,a-C) from 0.35 to 0.8 nm, in increments of 0.05 nm, the power-law slope (\u03b1) from \u221212 ...\n\nWe begin by conducting simulations using the DustEM code without radiative transfer, in order to produce a grid of THEMIS SEDs for G0 = 2.6 \u00d7 104. The grid covers variations in the minimum grain size (amin,a-C) from 0.35 to 0.8 nm, in increments of 0.05 nm, the power-law slope (\u03b1) from \u221212 to \u22125 with a step size of 0.25, and the hydrogenation state (Eg) from 0.01 to 0.1 eV. The modelled SED grid is then compared to three NIRSpec spectral templates corresponding to different regions in front of and inside the Bar (Peeters et al. 2024). These three spectra, shown in Fig. 6, correspond to (1) the ionized region in front of the Bar (top), (2) the atomic region of the Bar (middle), and (3) the warm molecular region (bottom), respectively. In comparing the spectra, we note variations in the 3.3 \u03bcm band (associated with aromatics) and the 3.4 \u03bcm band (associated with aliphatics), as well as differences in the band-to-continuum ratio.\n\nMethods. To characterize interstellar dust across the Orion Bar, we follow its emission as traced by JWST NIRCam (at 3.35 and 4.8 \u03bcm) and MIRI (at 7.7, 11.3, 15.0, and 25.5 \u03bcm) broad band images, along with NIRSpec and MRS spectroscopic observations. First, we constrain the minimum size and hydrogen content of carbon nano-grains from a comparison between the observed dust emission spectra and the predictions of the Heterogeneous dust Evolution Model for Interstellar Solids (THEMIS) coupled to the numerical code DustEM. Using this dust model, we then perform 3D radiative transfer simulations of dust emission with the SOC code (Scattering with OpenCL) and compare to data obtained along well chosen profiles across the Orion Bar. Results. The JWST data allows us, for the first time, to spatially resolve the steep variation of dust emission at the illuminated edge of the Orion Bar PDR.\n\nBright PDRs such as the Orion Bar are active regions where the star formation process is best studied (Hollenbach & Tielens 1999). Within the framework of an Early Release Science (ERS) observation program, the JWST space IR facility has observed gas and dust emissions towards the Orion Bar \u2013 the brightest PDR in the sky \u2013 at high angular resolution in imaging and spec-troscopy (Bern\u00e9 et al. 2022b; Habart et al. 2024; Peeters et al. 2024), providing an ideal data set to study the evolution of dust and its nature. Using these data, we analyse the dust emission across the Orion Bar PDR and compare it to radiative transfer simulations in order to derive the effect of dust evolution. The structure of the paper is as follows. In Sect. 2, we provide an overview of previous studies of the Orion Bar. We recall the JWST spectroscopic and photometric observations in Sect. 3. In Sect. 4, we detail the THEMIS dust model and the radiative transfer code used to simulate dust emission.\n\nThe edge of the Orion Bar PDR corresponds to the ionization front (IF), where the ionized gas (H II) recombines to neutral gas (H I). Right after this edge, photons have energies below 13.6 eV and the incident far-UV (FUV) radiation field is G0 = 2\u20137 \u00d7 104 times the Habing unit (e.g. Peeters et al. 2024). This atomic HI layer extends over 10\u201320\u2033 (or 0.02\u20130.04 pc) until dissociating FUV photons are sufficiently attenuated to let hydrogen become molecular (e.g. Habart et al. 2024) across a dissociation H/H2 front (DF). At this position the visual extinction to the IF is AV \u2243 0.5\u20132 mag. Beyond the DF, between AV = 2 and 4 mag, the C+/C/CO transition takes place (Tauber et al. 1995) and the PDR becomes molecular. Recently the ALMA and JWST images at high angular resolution (~0.1\u20131\u2033) have revealed small-scale (~0.004 pc) over-dense structures, sharp edges, and bright emission from embedded proplyds (object 203\u2013506; Champion et al.",

        "title": "Full SED fitting with the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR code - I. Dust modelling | Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A)",

        "text": "Aims. We revised the treatment of interstellar dust in the KOSMA-\u03c4 photo-dissociation region (PDR) model code to achieve a consistent description of the dust-related physics in the code. The detailed knowledge of the dust properties is then used to compute the dust continuum emission together ...\n\nAims. We revised the treatment of interstellar dust in the KOSMA-\u03c4 photo-dissociation region (PDR) model code to achieve a consistent description of the dust-related physics in the code. The detailed knowledge of the dust properties is then used to compute the dust continuum emission together with the line emission of chemical species. Methods. We coupled the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR code with the multi component dust radiative transfer (MCDRT) code to to solve the frequency-dependent radiative transfer equations and the thermal balance equation in a dusty clump under the assumption of spherical symmetry, assuming thermal equilibrium in calculating the dust temperatures, neglecting non-equilibrium effects. We updated the calculation of the photoelectric heating and extended the parametrization range for the photoelectric heating toward high densities and UV fields.\n\nThe rapid development in observational techniques over the last two decades left us with a vast amount of spectroscopic data that defies reproduction with simple numerical PDR models. We are far away from being able to really understand the conditions in any observed PDR (R\u00f6llig et al. 2007). In this paper we describe a number of steps that have been taken to increase the modeling power of the KOSMA-\u03c4 PDR model code. We tried to evolve the code toward a consistent treatment of dust physics and chemistry. By calculating the optical and energetic properties of a model cloud with a given dust composition we are now able to describe the full spectral emission characteristic of a model cloud including dust continuum and line emission1. The KOSMA-\u03c4 code (R\u00f6llig et al. 2006), a welltested and mature PDR model code (R\u00f6llig et al. 2007), applies spherical geometry to the problem of simultaneously solving the chemical and energy balance in an interstellar molecular cloud.\n\nKOSMA-\u03c4 is equipped with a modular chemical network, i.e., chemical species can easily be added or removed from the network and the network will rebuild dynamically, including isotopic chemistry of 13C and 18O. After obtaining the physical and chemical structure of the model cloud, a radiative transfer code is applied to calculate the resulting emissivities for the spectral line emission. Because of the spherical geometry of the model clump, radiation can reach a point inside the clump from any direction. The multi-component dust radiative transfer (MCDRT) code allows one to solve the frequency-dependent radiative transfer equations and the thermal balance equation in a dusty clump under the assumption of spherical symmetry (Yorke 1980). A detailed description of the code\u2019s main features is given in Szczerba et al. (1997). The code includes isotropic scattering, which is important at high optical depths.\n\nIn the following we describe how the new, full radiative transfer (RT) computation compares to the old approximation used in the KOSMA-\u03c4 model. For this purpose we evaluated a reference model clump with varying FUV radiative transfer and different dust models. We kept the following model parameters constant for all models: the total clump mass M = 10 M\u2299, the total surface gas density n = 105 cm-3, and the total FUV field \u03c7 = 1000 in units of the Draine field (Draine 1978). We applied a power law density gradient with power index 1.5 with a constant central gas density for radii smaller than Rtot/5. This implies Rtot = 2.46 \u00d7 1017 cm = 0.08 pc. We assumed a total cosmic ray ionization rate of molecular hydrogen \u03b6H2 = 5 \u00d7 10-17 s-1. The chemistry is based on the UMIST 2006 database for astrochemistry UDfA2 (Woodall et al. 2007), using 47 chemical species and 490 reactions in total.",

        "text": "KOSMA-tau is a numerical code to compute the physical and chemical structure of a spherical molecular cloud, a so called photo-dissociation region (PDR). This code has been developed from an earlier PDR code, written by A. Sternberg from Tel Aviv University in Israel (Sternberg &amp; Dalgarno 1989; ...\n\nKOSMA-tau is a numerical code to compute the physical and chemical structure of a spherical molecular cloud, a so called photo-dissociation region (PDR). This code has been developed from an earlier PDR code, written by A. Sternberg from Tel Aviv University in Israel (Sternberg & Dalgarno 1989; Sternberg & Dalgarno 1995). His original code uses a plane-parallel geometry and was updated to employ spherical geometry (Gierens, Stutzki and Winnewisser 1992; K\u00f6ster et al. 1994; St\u00f6rzer, Stutzki and Sternberg 1996; Zielinsky, Stutzki & St\u00f6rzer 2000). ... Chemistry: Solving the chemical problem is equivalent to solving a nonlinear system of rate equations, one equation for eacht chemical species that is includedin the model. The rate equation included all formation and destruction reactions of this particular species. Energy balance: Solving the energy balance means balancing all heating and cooling processes to derive the local kinetic gas (and dust) temperature.\n\ncomplex dust properties (e.g. Weingartner & Draine 2001 size distribution, full description in R\u00f6llig et al.",

        "text": "Keywords: Astrochemistry, Interstellar Medium, Dynamical Systems, Surrogate models, Machine Learning \u00b7 Computational models of the interstellar medium help us to understand the physical structure and chemical content that we observe in astronomical regions such as the Orion bar (Peeters et ...\n\nEfforts to gather different datasets and compare architectures are also being made (Janssen et al., 2024). The main goal of these surrogate models is to replace the plethora of computationally expensive astrochemical codes. The speedup of these surrogates enables the faster inference of observational results and the simulations of astronomical objects. With enough speedup, it could enable the direct inference of observations using coupled three-dimensional hydrodynamical and astrochemical codes, something which is currently prohibitively expensive. These coupled simulations are so expensive that they can currently only be run on university clusters and supercomputers (Seifried et al., 2017; Grudi\u0107 et al., 2021; Gong et al., 2023; Yue et al., 2024).         "text": "So far several machine learning based methods have been proposed to tackle problems in astrochemistry. Emulators have been trained to predict equilibrium chemistry (de Mijolla et al., 2019), time series based chemistry (Grassi et al., 2021; Holdship et al., 2021; Tang and Turk, 2022; Branca and Pallottini, 2022; Sulzer and Buck, 2023; Branca and Pallottini, 2024) and position based chemistry (Maes et al., 2024). But most of these have limited accuracy, only generalize to a small part of the physical parameter space or fail to include the interaction between temperature and chemistry. We then focus on benchmarking surrogate models (emulator) that work for large physical parameter spaces and are effective at emulating a selection of molecules that have complex formation pathways, without having to include the entire chemical network. To this end, we use Augmented Neural Ordinary equations ...\n\nSo far several machine learning based methods have been proposed to tackle problems in astrochemistry. Emulators have been trained to predict equilibrium chemistry (de Mijolla et al., 2019), time series based chemistry (Grassi et al., 2021; Holdship et al., 2021; Tang and Turk, 2022; Branca and Pallottini, 2022; Sulzer and Buck, 2023; Branca and Pallottini, 2024) and position based chemistry (Maes et al., 2024). But most of these have limited accuracy, only generalize to a small part of the physical parameter space or fail to include the interaction between temperature and chemistry. We then focus on benchmarking surrogate models (emulator) that work for large physical parameter spaces and are effective at emulating a selection of molecules that have complex formation pathways, without having to include the entire chemical network. To this end, we use Augmented Neural Ordinary equations (ANODE), where the augmented part of the NODE are auxiliary features that relate to the physical con\n\nMillar et al. [2024] T. J. Millar, C. Walsh, M. Van De Sande, and A. J. Markwick. The UMIST Database for Astrochemistry 2022. Astronomy & Astrophysics, 682:A109, February 2024. ISSN 0004-6361, 1432-0746. doi: 10.1051/0004-6361/202346908. Peeters et al. [2024] Els Peeters, Emilie Habart, Olivier Bern\u00e9, Ameek Sidhu, Ryan Chown, Dries Van De Putte, Boris Trahin, Ilane Schroetter, Am\u00e9lie Canin, Felipe Alarc\u00f3n, Bethany Schefter, Baria Khan, Sofia Pasquini, Alexander G. G. M. Tielens, Mark G. Wolfire, Emmanuel Dartois, Javier R. Goicoechea, Alexandros Maragkoudakis, Takashi Onaka, Marc W. Pound, S\u00edlvia Vicente, Alain Abergel, Edwin A. Bergin, Jeronimo Bernard-Salas, Christiaan Boersma, Emeric Bron, Jan Cami, Sara Cuadrado, Daniel Dicken, Meriem Elyajouri, Asunci\u00f3n Fuente, Karl D. Gordon, Lina Issa, Christine Joblin, Olga Kannavou, Ozan Lacinbala, David Languignon, Romane Le Gal, Raphael Meshaka, Yoko Okada, Massimo Robberto, Markus R\u00f6llig, Thi\u00e9baut Schirmer, Benoit Tabone, Marion Zannese, Is\n\nIn order to simulate this accurately, we need an iterative method that solves for heating, cooling and chemistry, creating a visual extinction based problem. The visual extinction is a measure for the decrease in radiation as we move into an astronomical object, and is related to the amount of hydrogen along a line of sight (G\u00fcver and \u00d6zel, 2009). By solving the differential equations describing these processes along a line of sight, we obtain the temperatures and abundances. Having to solve along lines of sight makes comprehensive 3D simulations of these regions computationally expensive, raising the need for surrogates that provide approximations of the chemistry, freeing up budget for hydrodynamics and radiative transport. In this article we provide a dataset inspired by the Orion Bar, a region within the Orion nebulae, that is currently being thoroughly investigated with the James Webb Space Telescope (Andree-Labsch et al., 2017; Habart et al., 2023; Peeters et al., 2024).\n\nEmulating the interstellar medium chemistry with neural operators, February 2024.         "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Neural-Network Chemical Emulator for First-Star Formation: Robust Iterative Predictions over a Wide Density Range",
        "url": "https://arxiv.org/html/2508.16114v1",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

        "text": "Maes et al. (2024) is specialized for a one-dimensional model of AGB outflows. None of these approaches is suitable for modeling chemical evolution during star formation, where physical states and timescales vary widely. Recently, L. Branca and A. Pallottini employed a physics-informed neural ...\n\nJ. Heyl et al. (2023) Heyl, J., Viti, S., & Vermari\u00ebn, G. 2023, \\bibinfotitleA statistical and machine learning approach to the study of astrochemistry, Faraday Discussions, 245, 569, doi: 10.1039/D3FD00008G \u00b7 A. E. Hoerl & R. W. Kennard (1970a) Hoerl, A. E., & Kennard, R. W. 1970a, \\bibinfotitleRidge regression: Biased estimation for nonorthogonal problems, Technometrics, 12, 55 \u00b7 A. E. Hoerl & R.\n\nA growing number of studies have been exploring the application of ML to emulate thermochemical evolution. After the seminal work in this direction by T. Grassi et al. (2011), dimensionality reduction techniques using autoencoders have been widely studied (J. Holdship et al., 2021; T. Grassi et al., 2022; I. Sulzer & T. Buck, 2023; S. Maes et al., 2024). In related fields, ML has also been studied to improve the interpretation of ISM observations (D. de Mijolla et al., 2019; J. Heyl et al., 2023; P. Palud et al., 2023) and to model networks of nuclear reactions inside stars (A. Grichener et al., 2025). Yet, the above autoencoder-based ML methods have been applied to limited situations: J. Holdship et al. (2021) developed an emulator for a fixed time interval; T. Grassi et al. (2022) and I. Sulzer & T. Buck (2023) constructed models for fixed density and temperature; and the model by S.\n\nMaes et al. (2024) is specialized for a one-dimensional model of AGB outflows. None of these approaches is suitable for modeling chemical evolution during star formation, where physical states and timescales vary widely. Recently, L. Branca and A. Pallottini employed a physics-informed neural network (PINN) (M. Raissi et al., 2019) in L. Branca & A. Pallottini (2023), and later adopted deep operator network (DeepONet) (L. Lu et al., 2021a) in L. Branca & A. Pallottini (2024) to model the evolution of the interstellar medium (ISM) in a more general setup. The latter study achieved higher accuracy than the former, reporting both accurate predictions and a speed-up of up to 128 times compared to traditional methods. However, P. van de Bor et al. (2025) found that the method presented in L. Branca & A. Pallottini (2024) is prone to instability due to the accumulation of prediction errors.\n\nThe problem of iterative use of emulators has been recognized and partially addressed in the context of autoencoder-based methods. This issue has been mitigated either by injecting Gaussian noise during training (J. Holdship et al., 2021) or by training the iterative updates themselves (S. Maes et al., 2024). Moreover, no solution has yet been proposed for the method of L. Branca & A. Pallottini (2024), even though numerous iterations are inevitable when applying ML methods to hydrodynamic simulations. Moreover, before applying the method of L. Branca & A. Pallottini (2024) to the case of star formation, another major challenge remains. While their model is not constrained by the aforementioned limitation of previous autoencoder-based ML methods (e.g., J. Holdship et al., 2021; T. Grassi et al., 2022; I. Sulzer & T. Buck, 2023; S. Maes et al., 2024), it still covers only low-density regimes typical of the ISM.",

        "text": "In this case, deriving a surrogate model thus becomes a regression problem, which benefits from many recent advances in numerical optimization developed for machine learning. Such approaches have already been applied in ISM studies. For instance, in Smirnov-Pinchukov et al. (2022), a k-nearest-neighbor regression algorithm was used to emulate a protoplanetary disks model, while in Bron et al. (2021), a random forest was trained to emulate a chemistry model. However, most often, the versatile class of artificial neural networks (ANNs) is preferred to address the complexity of comprehensive ISM models. For instance, ANN emulators of astrochemical models were derived in (de Mijolla et al. 2019; Holdship et al. 2021; Grassi et al. 2022). In addition, in Grassi et al. (2011), the authors derived a new simulation code and an associated ANN emulator. Here, we emulate a state-of-the- art ISM code, namely, the Meudon PDR code (Le Petit et al.\n\nResults. We compare the proposed ANNs with four standard classes of interpolation methods, nearest-neighbor, linear, spline, and radial basis function (RBF), to emulate a representative ISM numerical model known as the Meudon PDR code. Combinations of the proposed strategies produce networks that outperform all interpolation methods in terms of accuracy by a factor of 2 in terms of the average error (reaching 4.5% on the Meudon PDR code) and a factor of 3 for the worst-case errors (33%). These networks are also 1000 times faster than accurate interpolation methods and require ten to forty times less memory. Conclusions. This work will enable efficient inferences on wide-field multiline observations of the ISM.
