We note that ANNs come with the ability to automatically and efficiently compute derivatives such as the gradient and the Hessian matrix, which enables using faster and more accurate inference methods.         "text": "In this work we present surrogate models that can replace the original chemical code, namely Latent Augmented Neural Ordinary Differential Equations. We train these surrogate architectures on three datasets of increasing physical complexity, ...\n\nComputational astrochemical models are essential for helping us interpret and understand the observations of different astrophysical environments. In the age of high-resolution telescopes such as JWST and ALMA, the substructure of many objects can be resolved, raising the need for astrochemical modeling at these smaller scales, meaning that the simulations of these objects need to include both the physics and chemistry to accurately model the observations. The computational cost of the simulations coupling both the three-dimensional hydrodynamics and chemistry is enormous, creating an opportunity for surrogate models that can effectively substitute the chemical solver. In this work we present surrogate models that can replace the original chemical code, namely Latent Augmented Neural Ordinary Differential Equations. We train these surrogate architectures on three datasets of increasing physical complexity, with the last dataset derived directly from a three-dimensional simulation of a \n\nAbstract page for arXiv paper 2506.14270: NeuralPDR: Neural Differential Equations as surrogate models for Photodissociation Regions\n\nIn this work we present surrogate models that can replace the original chemical code, namely Latent Augmented Neural Ordinary Differential Equations. We train these surrogate architectures on three datasets of increasing physical complexity, with the last dataset derived directly from a three-dimensional simulation of a molecular cloud using a Photodissociation Region (PDR) code, 3D-PDR. We show that these surrogate models can provide speedup and reproduce the original observable column density maps of the dataset. This enables the rapid inference of the chemistry (on the GPU), allowing for the faster statistical inference of observations or increasing the resolution in hydrodynamical simulations of astrophysical environments.         "title": "fast neural emulator for interstellar chemistry | Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society | Oxford Academic",
        "url": "https://academic.oup.com/mnras/article/531/4/4930/7697560",
        "metadata": {

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "We are not the first team to explore emulators in chemistry. The vast majority of accurate enough emulators are based on neural networks, although other methods like random forest regression have been applied (Bron et al. 2021). de Mijolla et al. (2019) used a neural network to emulate astrochemical models in equilibrium after 107 yr obtained with UCLCHEM (Holdship et al. 2017). Holdship et al. (2021) trained a neural network to predict the change in abundance during a small time-step, allowing the user to evolve the chemistry for long times by iteratively applying the neural network. Grassi et al. (2022) and Sulzer &amp; Buck (2023) used auto-encoders to project large chemical networks into a low-dimensional latent space, where the chemistry is potentially evolved faster. A similar approach is followed by Maes et al. (2024) for emulating the chemistry in dynamical environments.\n\nAs a feasible approximation, an alternative method based on the usage of virtual particles has been used. These particles store the key parameters required for the computation of the chemistry (namely temperature and density) and a full state-of-the-art chemical model is then used to predict their chemical composition as a function of time (Hincelin et al. 2016; Coutens, Commer\u00e7on & Wakelam 2020; Navarro-Almaida et al. 2024). Yet, these calculations are expensive in terms of computing time since a large number of particles is needed to sample all the protostellar components and only a handful of cases have been completed, thus far. Consequently, a full comparison of these 3D chemo-MHD simulations with observations has not been done yet, so that their predictive power remains largely unexplored. Astrochemistry has become a necessary tool for understanding the interstellar medium.\n\nNeural networks are often described as black boxes in the sense that, even though they can approximate the results well, the structure of the neural network itself generally does not provide any insight into the model being approximated. It is a reality (although the machine learning community is actively working towards solving this issue) that it is hard to understand the learned relationship between the input parameters and the outcome of the model. This information is sometimes of great interest in astrochemistry, as questions like what physical conditions favour or hinder the production of a given chemical species are frequently faced. A potential avenue to understand the relationship between inputs and outputs in astrochemical models was addressed in Heyl, Butterworth & Viti (2023) using Shapley values (Shapley 1953). The Shapley value is an approach from game theory that provides a way to fairly distribute the outcome of a cooperative game among the players.\n\nAstrochemistry is a fundamental tool for the interpretation of observations in the interstellar medium. The solution to the system of nonlinear coupled ordinary differential equations describing the time-dependent chemistry in large chemical networks is feasible nowadays but requires lots of computing power. Having access to a fast emulator will open the possibility of improving our understanding of the physical conditions in the interstellar medium. We have developed such an emulator making use of NFs conditioned on the physical conditions and trained with computations carried out with the Nautilus code. We have demonstrated, through validation studies, that the emulator does an excellent job in approximating the time evolution of 192 species. The logarithm of the abundance of almost all species can be obtained with uncertainties well below 0.2 dex for all times below 107 yr.\n\nWe are not the first team to explore emulators in chemistry. The vast majority of accurate enough emulators are based on neural networks, although other methods like random forest regression have been applied (Bron et al. 2021). de Mijolla et al. (2019) used a neural network to emulate astrochemical models in equilibrium after 107 yr obtained with UCLCHEM (Holdship et al. 2017). Holdship et al. (2021) trained a neural network to predict the change in abundance during a small time-step, allowing the user to evolve the chemistry for long times by iteratively applying the neural network. Grassi et al. (2022) and Sulzer & Buck (2023) used auto-encoders to project large chemical networks into a low-dimensional latent space, where the chemistry is potentially evolved faster. A similar approach is followed by Maes et al. (2024) for emulating the chemistry in dynamical environments.",

        "text": "Context. The study of galaxy formation and evolution critically depends on our understanding of the complex photo-chemical processes that govern the evolution and thermodynamics of the interstellar medium (ISM). In a computational sense, resolving the chemistry is among the weightiest tasks in cosmological and astrophysical simulations. Aims: Astrophysical simulations can include photo-chemical models that allow for a wide range of densities (n), abundances of different species (ni/n) and temperature (T), and plausible evolution scenarios of the ISM under the action of a radiation field (F) with different spectral shapes and intensities. The evolution of such a non-equilibrium photo-chemical network relies on implicit, precise, computationally costly, ordinary differential equations (ODE) solvers. Here, we aim to substitute such procedural solvers with fast, pre-trained emulators based on neural operators.\n\nContext. The study of galaxy formation and evolution critically depends on our understanding of the complex photo-chemical processes that govern the evolution and thermodynamics of the interstellar medium (ISM). In a computational sense, resolving the chemistry is among the weightiest tasks in cosmological and astrophysical simulations. Aims: Astrophysical simulations can include photo-chemical models that allow for a wide range of densities (n), abundances of different species (ni/n) and temperature (T), and plausible evolution scenarios of the ISM under the action of a radiation field (F) with different spectral shapes and intensities. The evolution of such a non-equilibrium photo-chemical network relies on implicit, precise, computationally costly, ordinary differential equations (ODE) solvers. Here, we aim to substitute such procedural solvers with fast, pre-trained emulators based on neural operators.\n\n2024A&A...684A.203B \u00b7 Keywords: astrochemistry; methods: numerical; ISM: clouds; evolution; ISM: molecules; Astrophysics - Astrophysics of Galaxies; Computer Science - Machine Learning \u00b7 E-Print: 13 pages, 5 figures, Accepted for publication in A&amp \u00b7 full text sources \u00b7 Publisher \u00b7 | Preprint \u00b7 | Click to view more \u00b7",

        "text": "We present a neural-network emulator for the thermal and chemical evolution in Population III star formation. The emulator accurately reproduces the thermochemical evolution over a wide density range spanning 21 orders of magnitude ...\n\nWe present a neural-network emulator for the thermal and chemical evolution in Population III star formation. The emulator accurately reproduces the thermochemical evolution over a wide density range spanning 21 orders of magnitude (10$^{-3}$-10$^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$), tracking six primordial species: H, H$_2$, e$^{-}$, H$^{+}$, H$^{-}$, and H$_2^{+}$. To handle the broad dynamic range, we partition the density range into five subregions and train separate deep operator networks (DeepONets) in each region. When applied to randomly sampled thermochemical states, the emulator achieves relative errors below 10% in over 90% of cases for both temperature and chemical abundances (except for the rare species H$_2^{+}$). The emulator is roughly ten times faster on a CPU and more than 1000 times faster for batched predictions on a GPU, compared with conventional numerical integration.\n\nAbstract page for arXiv paper 2508.16114: Neural-Network Chemical Emulator for First-Star Formation: Robust Iterative Predictions over a Wide Density Range",

        "title": "Emulating the interstellar medium chemistry with neural operators | Astronomy & Astrophysics (A&A)",

        "text": "(2024), where cooling and heating tables from Gnedin &amp; Hollon (2012) were emulated. These tables were computed by assuming photo-ionization equilibrium conditions as a function of the incident radiation field; however, in Gnedin &amp; Hollon (2012) the radiation is approximated by adopting a limited ...\n\nKey words: astrochemistry / methods: numerical / ISM: clouds / evolution / ISM: molecules ... Open Access article, published by EDP Sciences, under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. This article is published in open access under the Subscribe to Open model. Subscribe to A&A to support open access publication. Non-equilibrium photo-chemistry plays a crucial role in many astrophysical and cosmological environments. Chemistry regulates the physical processes starting from cosmological scales (Galli & Palla 1998; Glover & Abel 2008) and it is key to understanding the evolution of the intergalactic medium (IGM) during the Epoch of Reionization (EoR, Theuns et al. 1998; Maio et al.\n\nFurthermore, an additional complication is given by the coupling between chemistry and radiation. Up to now, chemical emulators prepared for hydrodynamical coupling (Grassi et al. 2022; Branca & Pallottini 2023) allowed only for a fixed shape and intensity of the incident radiation. Instead, it ought to be feasible to fully pair a more refined model with an arbitrary radiation field, expressed, for instance, as a function of frequency; this is what would be required for a coupling appropriate for radiation hydrodynamic simulations (e.g. Rosdahl et al. 2018; Pallottini et al. 2019; Decataldo et al. 2020; Trebitsch et al. 2021; Katz 2022; Obreja et al. 2019, 2024). However, this adds a further degree of complexity to a problem that has not been fully explored thus far; indeed, to our knowledge, the only example of such exploration is given in Robinson et al.\n\nTherefore, we adopted the following method. First, we generated 64 flux values Fi in any of the bins in a range Fi \u220a [10\u221215, 10\u22125]eV cm\u22122 s\u22121 Hz\u22121. Then, we extracted a flux value in the adjacent bin such that |log(Fi/Fi\u00b11)| \u2264 0.15. In this way, the stochasticity of the incoming flows is maintained, without having completely non-physical spectra. In this way, we substantially reduced the size of the dataset, making it possible to refine the sampling of the initial conditions on the fractions. We note that imposing such a constraint for the flux is somewhat similar to the selection of a continuity prior in spectral energy density fitting when exploring non-parametric star formation rate histories by allowing for burstiness (Tacchella et al. 2020; Ciesla et al. 2024). The time evolution was evaluated in 16 random uniformly sampled points in the range of 0 \u2264 t/kyr \u2264 1.\n\n(2024), where cooling and heating tables from Gnedin & Hollon (2012) were emulated. These tables were computed by assuming photo-ionization equilibrium conditions as a function of the incident radiation field; however, in Gnedin & Hollon (2012) the radiation is approximated by adopting a limited number of photoionization rates. Thus, by construction, the model cannot yield the evolution of the ISM chemistry. To this end, a novel possibility consists of exploiting neural operators to emulate the photo-chemical ODEs system. The use of neural operators could be crucial in learning the differential operator that describes the chemical network, giving the emulator the flexibility required to be effectively used in simulations. In particular, in this work we want to explore the usage of a particular architecture, namely, deep neural operator (Deep-ONet), first introduced in Lu et al.",

      "file_text": "# PRISM-3D: Competitive Analysis & Updated Roadmap\n## Based on Literature Survey March 2026\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Competitive Landscape \u2014 Where Every Code Stands\n\n### Existing PDR Codes (as of 2025/2026)\n\n| Code | Geometry | Chemistry | Dust | RT | GPU | ML | Obs. Pipeline |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Meudon PDR** | 1D slab | ~300 species, full H\u2082 rovib | Coupled to DustEM/THEMIS | 1D \u03bb-dependent | \u274c | ANN emulator (Palud+ 2023) | Via PDR Toolbox |\n| **Cloudy** | 1D slab/sphere | Massive network (~600 sp) | Full grain physics | 1D multi-\u03bb | \u274c | \u274c | Built-in SED |\n| **KOSMA-\u03c4** | 1D sphere | ~47 species + surface chem | WD01 + MCDRT coupling | 1D spherical | \u274c | \u274c | Full SED (R\u00f6llig+ 2013) |\n| **3D-PDR** | 3D HEALPix | ~30 species | Basic | 3D multi-ray | \u274c | NeuralPDR (Vermari\u00ebn+ 2025) | Column density maps |\n| **PRISM-3D** | 3D regular | 75 reactions, 32 species | **Single grey (gap!)** | 3D multi-ray vectorized | \u2705 CUDA kernel | Planned | Basic maps |\n\n### Key Findings from Literature\n\n**1. JWST has changed everything (2022-2025):**\n  - PDRs4All (Bern\u00e9+ 2022) delivered resolved observations of Orion Bar\n    at 0.1\" resolution \u2014 individual dissociation fronts are RESOLVED\n  - Horsehead nebula: H\u2082 rovibrational lines spatially resolved (Zannese+ 2025)\n  - Dust evolution ACROSS the PDR front is now observable (Elyajouri+ 2024)\n  - PAH band ratios vary on scales of arcseconds (Peeters+ 2024)\n  - **Implication:** 1D codes CANNOT reproduce these resolved structures.\n    3D is essential. This is our opportunity.\n\n**2. Dust modeling is THE differentiator (2024-2025):**\n  - PDRs4All V (Elyajouri+ 2024): Used THEMIS + DustEM + SOC 3D RT\n    to model dust across the Orion Bar. First time dust evolution\n    (grain size changes, PAH destruction) is directly observed.\n  - Horsehead PDR III (2025): THEMIS + SOC for dust at the Horsehead.\n    They find grain properties CHANGE across the PDR front.\n  - **Implication:** Static dust models are dead. Need evolutionary dust.\n    But NO code does dust evolution self-consistently IN 3D with chemistry.\n\n**3. Neural network surrogates are coming (2024-2025):**\n  - NeuralPDR (Vermari\u00ebn+ 2025): Neural ODEs trained on 3D-PDR datasets.\n    Reproduce column density maps. GPU-fast. But limited to 3D-PDR's \n    reduced chemistry and DON'T include dust or thermal balance.\n  - Palud+ (2023): ANN emulator of Meudon PDR code. 1000x faster than\n    interpolation. But only emulates the 1D model \u2014 can't do 3D.\n  - Branca & Pallottini (2024): DeepONet for ISM chemistry with \n    radiation field coupling. 128x speedup. But numerically unstable.\n  - Ramos+ (2024): Normalizing flows for Nautilus chemistry emulator.\n    192 species. Fast. But no spatial context.\n  - **Implication:** ML surrogates work but are FRAGILE. They fail\n    on extrapolation and accumulate errors over iterations. \n    Our approach: use ML as an ACCELERATOR for the first guess,\n    then refine with physics. This is more robust than pure surrogates.\n\n**4. THEMIS dust model is becoming the standard:**\n  - Jones+ (2013, 2017): THEMIS = \"The Heterogeneous dust Evolution\n    Model for Interstellar Solids\" \u2014 models dust as evolving from\n    amorphous hydrocarbons to aromatic to aliphatic, with size-dependent\n    optical properties.\n  - Now coupled to Meudon PDR code via DustEM\n  - And to 3D Monte Carlo RT via SOC (Juvela+ 2003)\n  - **Implication:** We should interface with DustEM/THEMIS rather than\n    reinvent the wheel. Use their optical property tables.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Where PRISM-3D is AHEAD\n\n\u2705 **True 3D with vectorized RT** \u2014 3D-PDR uses HEALPix rays but loops in\n   Fortran. We use vectorized cumulative sums \u2014 fundamentally faster.\n   Our RT for 16\u00b3 grid: <0.01s. Their RT for similar: seconds.\n\n\u2705 **GPU chemistry kernel** \u2014 No other PDR code has native GPU chemistry.\n   3D-PDR is CPU-only. Meudon is serial. KOSMA-\u03c4 is serial.\n   Our CUDA kernel enables million-cell grids.\n\n\u2705 **Dual-mode solver** \u2014 Fast explicit for iteration, BDF for refinement.\n   No other code offers this adaptivity.\n\n\u2705 **Modern Python codebase** \u2014 Easy to extend, interface with ML frameworks\n   (PyTorch, JAX), and deploy on cloud/HPC. Most other codes are Fortran.\n\n\u2705 **Benchmark-validated 3D** \u2014 We have the R\u00f6llig benchmark passing AND\n   3D running. 3D-PDR has 3D but with reduced chemistry and no thermal\n   balance benchmark.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Where PRISM-3D is BEHIND (the gaps to fill)\n\n\u274c **Dust treatment** \u2014 THE biggest gap. Everyone else has evolved past\n   grey opacity. Meudon has DustEM. KOSMA-\u03c4 has MCDRT. Even 3D-PDR\n   has basic grain physics. We have a single cross-section.\n\n\u274c **H\u2082 ro-vibrational levels** \u2014 JWST measures individual H\u2082 lines.\n   Meudon has full ro-vib (250+ levels). We have a"

      "code": "# PRISM-3D: Competitive Analysis & Updated Roadmap\n## Based on Literature Survey March 2026\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Competitive Landscape \u2014 Where Every Code Stands\n\n### Existing PDR Codes (as of 2025/2026)\n\n| Code | Geometry | Chemistry | Dust | RT | GPU | ML | Obs. Pipeline |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Meudon PDR** | 1D slab | ~300 species, full H\u2082 rovib | Coupled to DustEM/THEMIS | 1D \u03bb-dependent | \u274c | ANN emulator (Palud+ 2023) | Via PDR Toolbox |\n| **Cloudy** | 1D slab/sphere | Massive network (~600 sp) | Full grain physics | 1D multi-\u03bb | \u274c | \u274c | Built-in SED |\n| **KOSMA-\u03c4** | 1D sphere | ~47 species + surface chem | WD01 + MCDRT coupling | 1D spherical | \u274c | \u274c | Full SED (R\u00f6llig+ 2013) |\n| **3D-PDR** | 3D HEALPix | ~30 species | Basic | 3D multi-ray | \u274c | NeuralPDR (Vermari\u00ebn+ 2025) | Column density maps |\n| **PRISM-3D** | 3D regular | 75 reactions, 32 species | **Single grey (gap!)** | 3D multi-ray vectorized | \u2705 CUDA kernel | Planned | Basic maps |\n\n### Key Findings from Literature\n\n**1. JWST has changed everything (2022-2025):**\n  - PDRs4All (Bern\u00e9+ 2022) delivered resolved observations of Orion Bar\n    at 0.1\" resolution \u2014 individual dissociation fronts are RESOLVED\n  - Horsehead nebula: H\u2082 rovibrational lines spatially resolved (Zannese+ 2025)\n  - Dust evolution ACROSS the PDR front is now observable (Elyajouri+ 2024)\n  - PAH band ratios vary on scales of arcseconds (Peeters+ 2024)\n  - **Implication:** 1D codes CANNOT reproduce these resolved structures.\n    3D is essential. This is our opportunity.\n\n**2. Dust modeling is THE differentiator (2024-2025):**\n  - PDRs4All V (Elyajouri+ 2024): Used THEMIS + DustEM + SOC 3D RT\n    to model dust across the Orion Bar. First time dust evolution\n    (grain size changes, PAH destruction) is directly observed.\n  - Horsehead PDR III (2025): THEMIS + SOC for dust at the Horsehead.\n    They find grain properties CHANGE across the PDR front.\n  - **Implication:** Static dust models are dead. Need evolutionary dust.\n    But NO code does dust evolution self-consistently IN 3D with chemistry.\n\n**3. Neural network surrogates are coming (2024-2025):**\n  - NeuralPDR (Vermari\u00ebn+ 2025): Neural ODEs trained on 3D-PDR datasets.\n    Reproduce column density maps. GPU-fast. But limited to 3D-PDR's \n    reduced chemistry and DON'T include dust or thermal balance.\n  - Palud+ (2023): ANN emulator of Meudon PDR code. 1000x faster than\n    interpolation. But only emulates the 1D model \u2014 can't do 3D.\n  - Branca & Pallottini (2024): DeepONet for ISM chemistry with \n    radiation field coupling. 128x speedup. But numerically unstable.\n  - Ramos+ (2024): Normalizing flows for Nautilus chemistry emulator.\n    192 species. Fast. But no spatial context.\n  - **Implication:** ML surrogates work but are FRAGILE. They fail\n    on extrapolation and accumulate errors over iterations. \n    Our approach: use ML as an ACCELERATOR for the first guess,\n    then refine with physics. This is more robust than pure surrogates.\n\n**4. THEMIS dust model is becoming the standard:**\n  - Jones+ (2013, 2017): THEMIS = \"The Heterogeneous dust Evolution\n    Model for Interstellar Solids\" \u2014 models dust as evolving from\n    amorphous hydrocarbons to aromatic to aliphatic, with size-dependent\n    optical properties.\n  - Now coupled to Meudon PDR code via DustEM\n  - And to 3D Monte Carlo RT via SOC (Juvela+ 2003)\n  - **Implication:** We should interface with DustEM/THEMIS rather than\n    reinvent the wheel. Use their optical property tables.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Where PRISM-3D is AHEAD\n\n\u2705 **True 3D with vectorized RT** \u2014 3D-PDR uses HEALPix rays but loops in\n   Fortran. We use vectorized cumulative sums \u2014 fundamentally faster.\n   Our RT for 16\u00b3 grid: <0.01s. Their RT for similar: seconds.\n\n\u2705 **GPU chemistry kernel** \u2014 No other PDR code has native GPU chemistry.\n   3D-PDR is CPU-only. Meudon is serial. KOSMA-\u03c4 is serial.\n   Our CUDA kernel enables million-cell grids.\n\n\u2705 **Dual-mode solver** \u2014 Fast explicit for iteration, BDF for refinement.\n   No other code offers this adaptivity.\n\n\u2705 **Modern Python codebase** \u2014 Easy to extend, interface with ML frameworks\n   (PyTorch, JAX), and deploy on cloud/HPC. Most other codes are Fortran.\n\n\u2705 **Benchmark-validated 3D** \u2014 We have the R\u00f6llig benchmark passing AND\n   3D running. 3D-PDR has 3D but with reduced chemistry and no thermal\n   balance benchmark.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Focus especially on papers from 2023-2025 and any developments in 2026."

    "input": {
      "id": "compass_artifact_wf-09e53b36-6c5b-440a-b308-76a0f54f0918_text/markdown",
      "type": "text/markdown",
      "title": "Competitive Analysis for PRISM-3D: The Path to a World-Leading PDR Code in the JWST Era",
      "source": "c",
      "command": "create",
      "content": "# Competitive analysis for PRISM-3D: the path to a world-leading PDR code\n\n**No publicly available code simultaneously handles full 3D geometry, detailed H\u2082 rovibrational physics, comprehensive surface chemistry, self-consistent dust evolution, and JWST-grade spectral predictions.** This gap \u2014 confirmed by surveying every major PDR code active in 2020\u20132026 \u2014 defines the opportunity for PRISM-3D. The JWST era has exposed fundamental limitations of 1D models: the Orion Bar's \"terraced\" multi-front geometry, **15\u00d7 nano-grain depletion** at PDR edges, and over-pressurized H\u2082 gas that stationary models cannot explain all demand a 3D, time-dependent approach with evolved dust physics. PRISM-3D can occupy this empty niche by combining the microphysical rigor of the Meudon PDR code with the 3D architecture of 3D-PDR, the THEMIS dust evolution framework, and ML-accelerated chemistry \u2014 a combination no competitor has attempted.\n\n---\n\n## The competitive landscape: five codes, one critical vacancy\n\nThe PDR modeling community relies on roughly five mature codes, each dominant in one dimension but deficient in others. **No genuinely new PDR code has appeared since 2022**, and no existing code has been redesigned for the JWST era.\n\n**Meudon PDR code** (v7.1, October 2025) remains the gold standard for microphysics. It computes full H\u2082 and HD rovibrational level populations including UV pumping, collisional excitation, and formation pumping, with DustEM coupling for grain IR emission. The PDRs4All collaboration uses it as their primary theoretical engine, producing landmark results like the CH\u2083\u207a detection (Bern\u00e9 et al. 2023, *Nature*) and HD rovibrational emission modeling (Zannese et al. 2026). Its fatal limitation is **1D plane-parallel geometry** \u2014 it cannot model the terraced, multi-front structure JWST revealed in the Orion Bar. THEMIS dust integration remains incomplete (Meshaka et al., in prep.).\n\n**KOSMA-\u03c4** (R\u00f6llig & Ossenkopf-Okada 2022) offers a unique spherical clumpy ensemble approach where molecular clouds are represented as collections of Bonnor-Ebert-like clumps with a mass spectrum. Its 2022 update introduced full surface chemistry with chemical desorption branching ratios and multi-component dust radiative transfer (MCDRT) using Weingartner & Draine size distributions. However, it remains **1D spherical**, lacks full H\u2082 rovibrational treatment (in preparation), and does not include stochastic heating of very small grains.\n\n**3D-PDR** (Bisbas et al. 2012) is the only true 3D PDR code available, using HEALPix ray-tracing to compute column densities and FUV attenuation along uniformly distributed rays from each cell. But it pays for dimensionality with **drastically simplified physics**: escape probability radiative transfer, reduced chemical networks (33\u2013128 species), no detailed H\u2082 rovibrational cascades, and minimal dust treatment. Its extension TORUS-3DPDR (Bisbas et al. 2015) couples Monte Carlo ionizing radiation with HEALPix PDR chemistry on AMR grids, but still uses reduced chemistry.\n\n**Cloudy** (C25.00, Ferland group) possesses the broadest microphysical scope of any spectral synthesis code, seamlessly modeling HII\u2192PDR\u2192molecular transitions. Its 2025 release added SiS rovibrational levels, updated CHIANTI v10, and LAMDA molecular data. Yet Cloudy is **1D only**, its dust-gas depletion is not self-consistent, and its molecular chemistry is less detailed than dedicated PDR codes.\n\n**PDR Toolbox** (pdrtpy v2.4.4, Pound & Wolfire 2023) is not a physics code but an observation-comparison framework using pre-computed model grids (Wolfire-Kaufman 2020 and KOSMA-\u03c4) to derive density *n* and radiation field *G\u2080* via \u03c7\u00b2 minimization over line ratios. It now supports MCMC fitting, H\u2082 excitation diagrams, and pixel-by-pixel parameter mapping, making it the standard observer tool \u2014 but it is limited by its underlying 1D grids.\n\nThe competitive vacancy is clear: **the intersection of 3D geometry + Meudon-grade microphysics + THEMIS dust evolution + time dependence** is unoccupied.\n\n---\n\n## JWST has broken every 1D PDR model\n\nThe PDRs4All Early Release Science program (Bern\u00e9, Habart, Peeters; JWST ERS #1288) has produced **21+ numbered papers** through early 2026, fundamentally reshaping our understanding of PDR physics and exposing where models fail.\n\nThe most consequential discovery is geometric: Habart et al. (2024) revealed that the Orion Bar's H/H\u2082 dissociation front is not a single surface but a **\"terraced\" distribution of three distinct dissociation fronts** (DF1, DF2, DF3) \u2014 a complex, folded 3D structure at scales of 40\u2013400 AU that no 1D model can represent. Peeters et al. (2024) detected a \"forest of lines\" in NIRSpec IFU spectra including H\u2082 rotational and rovibrational transitions, HD, CO, CH\u207a, CH\u2083\u207a, and nearly **7,000 catalogued lines and dust bands** \u2014 most observed for the first time in a PDR.\n\nSpecific model failures are now well documented. Van De Putte et al. (2024) showed the Meudon PDR code requires **parameter adjustments to reproduce H\u2082 0-0 S(6)\u2013S(8) lines**, with the non-thermal UV-pumped component (~2700 K) difficult to capture fully. In the Horsehead, Zannese et al. (2025) found that **stationary 1D models cannot account for the 2D spatial separation between FUV-pumped and thermalized H\u2082 lines**, nor the thermal pressure exceeding **6 \u00d7 10\u2076 K cm\u207b\u00b3** (10\u00d7 higher than ionized gas). This over-pressurized gas likely originates from dynamical mixing during photoevaporation \u2014 physics entirely absent from equilibrium codes. Perhaps most critically, Elyajouri et al. (2024) demonstrated that carbonaceous nano-grains are **depleted by a factor of 15** at the Orion Bar's illuminated edge relative to the diffuse ISM, invalidating every PDR gas model that assumes constant dust properties.\n\nJWST's specifications create precise demands on models. NIRSpec IFU achieves **0.075\u2033\u20130.173\u2033 angular resolution** (30\u201370 AU at 414 pc) at R~2700; MIRI MRS provides **R~1500\u20133500** from 4.9\u201327.9 \u03bcm. Signal-to-noise ratios routinely exceed 200\u20131000 per spaxel, meaning **observational uncertainties are now smaller than systematic model uncertainties** (Van De Putte et al. 2025). Models must resolve structures at ~100 AU scales, predict individual rovibrational line intensities at R~1000+, and track grain evolution as a function of position.\n\nThe Horsehead (GTO #1192) and NGC 7023 complement the Orion Bar at lower radiation fields. The Horsehead's moderate G\u2080 ~ 100 produces less extreme nano-grain depletion than the Orion Bar's G\u2080 ~ 2\u20137 \u00d7 10\u2074, with nano-grains on average **larger and less UV-processed** (Elyajouri et al. 2025). Misselt et al. (2025) detected complete removal of aliphatic emission in the Horsehead's photoevaporative outflow, directly tracing progressive UV processing from aliphatic to aromatic character. NGC 7023 (G\u2080 ~ 1000) sits between the two extremes, offering intermediate PAH and fluorescence diagnostics.\n\n---\n\n## THEMIS + DustEM + SOC: the dust implementation blueprint\n\nThe state-of-the-art dust modeling pipeline, demonstrated by Elyajouri et al. (2024, 2025), combines three codes that PRISM-3D should integrate natively rather than as separate post-processing steps.\n\n**THEMIS** (The Heterogeneous dust Evolution Model for Interstellar Solids; Jones et al. 2013, 2017; THEMIS 2.0: Ysard et al. 2024) replaces the static Weingartner & Draine (2001) grain model with a physically motivated evolutionary framework. Its key innovation is treating carbonaceous dust as a continuous family of hydrogenated amorphous carbons, a-C(:H), parameterized by the band gap *E*_g \u2014 low *E*_g material is aromatic-rich (UV-processed), high *E*_g is aliphatic-rich (shielded). Dust evolves through four stages: CM (core/mantle, diffuse ISM) \u2192 CMM (secondary a-C:H mantle accretion) \u2192 AMM (grain coagulation) \u2192 AMMI (ice mantle accretion in dense regions). Unlike WD01, which treats PAHs as a separate static population, THEMIS produces aromatic infrared bands naturally from size-dependent optical properties of a-C nano-particles. THEMIS 2.0 updated silicate optical constants using laboratory measurements by Demyk et al. and added spheroidal grain calculations for polarized emission.\n\n**DustEM** (Compi\u00e8gne et al. 2011) computes grain temperature distributions P(T), emission spectra, extinction, and grain charging in the optically thin limit. For nano-grains (radius \u2272 10 nm), it solves the full stochastic heating problem where single UV photon absorption causes temperature spikes of hundreds of kelvin. It provides photoelectric heating rates and recombination cooling rates self-consistently for the THEMIS grain population. DustEM already interfaces with the Meudon PDR code for grain physics.\n\n**SOC** (Juvela 2019) performs 3D Monte Carlo dust continuum radiative transfer on octree grids with GPU acceleration, solving for dust temperatures and producing synthetic observations at arbitrary viewing angles. It converts DustEM grain files to its internal format. The Elyajouri et al. pipeline runs SOC with THEMIS optical properties to compute the radiation field at each position, then DustEM to evaluate local emission \u2014 producing synthetic photometric maps directly comparable to JWST NIRCam and MIRI data.\n\nFor PRISM-3D, the recommended implementation strategy involves three tiers:\n\n- **Tier 1 (essential)**: Integrate THEMIS grain optical properties and size distributions directly into the radiative transfer module. Compute photoelectric heating, grain charging, and H\u2082 formation rates self-consistently for the local grain population at each cell, using DustEM-derived physics. This replaces the standard WD01 parameterizations used by 3D-PDR and most other codes.\n- **Tier 2 (differentiating)**: Implement position-dependent dust evolution \u2014 track the local nano-grain abundance, minimum grain size, and band gap *E*_g as functions of the radiation field history and density. The JWST results show these vary by factors of 3\u201315 across PDR interfaces. This requires solving for grain processing timescales (photo-destruction: ~10\u00b2\u201310\u2074 yr for small a-C nanoparticles in PDRs; photo-fragmentation of aggregates replenishing nano-grains; dehydrogenation/aromatization timescales).\n- **Tier 3 (world-leading)**: Couple dust evolution feedback to gas physics \u2014 nano-grain depletion reduces photoelectric heating, H\u2082 formation surface area, and UV opacity simultaneously, shifting the C\u207a/C/CO transition zone and altering temperature profiles. Preliminary Meudon PDR simulations (Meshaka et al., in prep.) show this affects H\u2082 emission line ratios. No existing PDR code does this self-consistently in 3D.\n\n---\n\n## ML-accelerated chemistry: promising but not production-ready\n\nThe ML astrochemistry field has produced ~10 distinct approaches since 2021, with a clear trajectory from proof-of-concept to near-production tools. The most relevant for PRISM-3D are:\n\n**Palud et al. (2023, A&A)** trained dense neural networks on 19,208 Meudon PDR models to create a static emulator mapping physical parameters (P_th, G_UV, A_V, viewing angle) directly to 5,375 line intensities with **4.5% average error** and 1000\u00d7 speedup. This is already production-ready for Bayesian inference \u2014 their Beetroots framework (Palud et al. 2025) performs spatially-regularized MCMC on wide-field multiline observations using the emulator for likelihood evaluation. PRISM-3D should produce model grids compatible with this inference pipeline.\n\n**NeuralPDR** (Vermari\u00ebn et al. 2025, accepted in *ML: Science and Technology*) uses Latent Augmented Neural ODEs as surrogates for 3D-PDR's chemical solver, operating in an encoder-evolve-decode architecture where the neural ODE evolves abundances through a compressed latent space. It reproduces observable column density maps but struggles with low-abundance species and numerical artifacts from HEALPix ray-tracing.\n\n**Branca & Pallottini (2024, A&A)** demonstrated DeepONet for ISM chemistry with **arbitrary radiation fields** \u2014 a critical advance for coupling with RT \u2014 achieving ~1% accuracy and 128\u00d7 speedup on a 9-species network. However, the architecture favors smooth solutions and handles abrupt transitions (ionization fronts, H/H\u2082 boundaries) poorly.\n\nThe **central obstacle for production deployment is error accumulation** during iterative application, as demonstrated by van de Bor et al. (2025). When ML predictions replace the ODE solver at each hydrodynamic timestep, small errors compound and can drive solutions to unphysical states. The most promising fix comes from **Ono & Sugimura (2025)**, who introduce a timescale-based update method: the ML prediction is suppressed when the predicted change falls below the characteristic evolution timescale, preventing noise-dominated evolution. Combined with integrated multi-step training (Maes et al. 2024, MACE) and noise injection during training (Holdship et al. 2021), this brings ML emulators close to viability.\n\nFor PRISM-3D, the pragmatic strategy is a **hybrid architecture**: use traditional stiff ODE solvers (SUNDIALS/DVODE) as the default, with ML surrogates activated for cells in common parameter regimes where accuracy is validated. GPU-batched neural network inference across all cells simultaneously offers natural parallelism. Conservation laws should be enforced as hard post-processing constraints. The ML pathway is best deployed first for parameter inference (Palud-style emulation of PRISM-3D grids) rather than replacing the internal chemistry solver.\n\n---\n\n## 3D radiative transfer: TreeRay and beyond\n\nThe choice of 3D radiative transfer method determines PRISM-3D's computational scaling and accuracy ceiling. Three approaches dominate the field, each with distinct trade-offs.\n\n**HEALPix ray-tracing** (used by 3D-PDR) creates uniformly distributed rays from each cell center, computing column densities along each direction for FUV attenuation and escape probability line emission. This is conceptually straightforward and handles arbitrary density distributions, but computational cost scales linearly with the number of HEALPix pixels (angular resolution) and the number of cells traversed per ray. For high angular resolution, this becomes prohibitive.\n\n**TreeRay** (W\u00fcnsch et al. 2021, implemented in FLASH) combines reverse ray-tracing with tree-based integration, making the cost **independent of the number of radiation sources** \u2014 a major advantage for PDR environments with distributed UV fields. Its OpticalDepth module computes column densities from each cell to the boundary during the tree-walk, and the OnTheSpot sub-module handles EUV ionizing radiation. TreeRay supports HEALPix angular discretization, achieves nearly ideal parallel scaling, and has been extended to X-rays (Gaches et al. 2023). This is the most promising RT framework for PRISM-3D if built on FLASH or a similar tree-solver infrastructure.\n\n**The six-ray approximation** (Glover & Mac Low 2007) computes column densities along only six coordinate-axis directions, using the minimum for UV shielding. Despite its crudeness, it has been enormously successful in large-scale ISM simulations (SILCC, TIGRESS) because the computational overhead is minimal. SILCC-Zoom (Seifried et al. 2017\u20132025) achieves 0.06 pc resolution on AMR grids with a 6-species Glover network, demonstrating that simplified chemistry with adequate RT can capture molecular cloud formation and CO-dark gas. However, the angular resolution is far too coarse for detailed PDR modeling.\n\nFor PRISM-3D to be \"world-leading,\" it needs **TreeRay-class RT** (or better) combined with chemical networks 10\u201350\u00d7 larger than SILCC's 6 species. AMR is essential \u2014 PDR fronts occur over ~0.01\u20130.1 pc embedded in clouds spanning 10\u2013100 pc, and SILCC-Zoom demonstrated that **\u22640.1 pc resolution is required for convergence** of chemical abundances. Refinement criteria should track temperature gradients and chemical timescales to resolve the C\u207a/C/CO transition dynamically.\n\n---\n\n## Making results visually stunning and scientifically compelling\n\nThe visualization strategy should target three audiences simultaneously: journal referees (rigorous comparison panels), conference attendees (cinematic 3D renders), and the broader community (synthetic JWST-style images).\n\nCurrent best practices in the field include column density projections in multiple tracers side-by-side (SILCC-Zoom standard), phase diagrams weighted by molecular mass fraction, and synthetic emission maps post-processed through RADMC-3D for line RT or POLARIS for dust polarization. The most impactful recent figures combine **model-observation comparison panels** at matched angular resolution \u2014 the Elyajouri et al. (2024) THEMIS+SOC synthetic images placed beside JWST NIRCam/MIRI photometry represent the current standard for dust work.\n\nThe tools ecosystem centers on **yt-project** (Turk et al. 2011) for analysis and rendering of AMR simulation data, with native support for FLASH, Enzo, RAMSES, and Athena. yt provides software volume rendering with configurable transfer functions, supporting standard, dome, and VR outputs. For publication-quality 3D renders, **Houdini** (following the Naiman et al. 2017 and Borkiewicz et al. 2019 workflow) produces cinematic results by importing yt-converted data. **ParaView** handles parallel rendering on HPC systems with in-situ visualization via Catalyst. **POLARIS** (Reissl et al. 2016) generates synthetic dust polarization maps showing magnetic field morphology overlaid on intensity.\n\nFor maximum impact, PRISM-3D should produce:\n- **Synthetic JWST scenes**: Forward-modeled NIRCam and MIRI images including PSF convolution, noise, and spatial sampling \u2014 directly overlaid on real PDRs4All data\n- **Chemical stratification movies**: Time evolution of the atomic\u2192molecular transition across a 3D PDR, rendered as transparent outer layers (C\u207a) revealing dense molecular cores (CO), with H\u2082 rovibrational emission highlighted at dissociation fronts\n- **Multi-tracer comparison panels**: The same 3D model viewed in [CII] 158 \u03bcm, [OI] 63 \u03bcm, H\u2082 1-0 S(1), CO(1-0), and 3.3 \u03bcm PAH emission, each highlighting different PDR layers\n- **Interactive web-based 3D viewers** using X3D format (Vogt et al. 2016, 2017) embedded in publications, allowing readers to rotate and slice through the chemistry\n\n---\n\n## Observation comparison: from \u03c7\u00b2 to Bayesian parameter maps\n\nThe path from model to validated code runs through three progressively sophisticated comparison methodologies.\n\n**Tier 1 \u2014 R\u00f6llig benchmark reproduction**: The 2007 multi-code benchmark (R\u00f6llig et al., A&A 467, 187) defined eight test problems (F1\u2013F4 with fixed temperature, V1\u2013V4 with thermal balance) spanning densities of 10\u00b3\u201310\u2075\u00b7\u2075 cm\u207b\u00b3 and FUV fields of 10\u201310\u2075. Approximately 13 codes participated. PRISM-3D should reproduce these benchmarks as a first validation step, noting that the original comparison revealed significant spread driven by PAH treatment, H\u2082 formation rates, and UV attenuation differences. No updated multi-code benchmark has been published since 2007.\n\n**Tier 2 \u2014 PDR Toolbox integration and line ratio validation**: PRISM-3D should produce model grids in pdrtpy-compatible format, enabling direct comparison against Wolfire-Kaufman 2020 and KOSMA-\u03c4 predictions for standard diagnostic ratios: [CII]158/[OI]63, ([CII]+[OI])/I_FIR, CO ladders, and H\u2082 excitation diagrams. The three benchmark PDRs provide distinct validation regimes \u2014 the Orion Bar (G\u2080 ~ 2\u20137 \u00d7 10\u2074, n ~ 10\u2075 cm\u207b\u00b3), Horsehead (G\u2080 ~ 100, density gradient from 10\u00b2 to 2 \u00d7 10\u2075 cm\u207b\u00b3), and NGC 7023 (G\u2080 ~ 1000, clumpy filamentary structure).\n\n**Tier 3 \u2014 Bayesian inference on JWST data**: The Palud et al. (2025) Beetroots framework represents the frontier: spatially-regularized MCMC using neural network emulators of PDR code output, with posterior predictive p-values for model checking. This approach automatically flags regions where the model cannot explain observations. PRISM-3D's competitive advantage would be providing the **first 3D model grids for Bayesian inference**, replacing the plane-parallel assumption that limits current parameter estimation. The key degeneracies in PDR fitting \u2014 the n\u2013G\u2080 degeneracy along constant G\u2080/n contours, geometry effects (plane-parallel vs. spherical vs. clumpy), and [OI] 63 \u03bcm optical depth \u2014 can all be partially broken by 3D models that predict spatial profiles rather than just integrated intensities.\n\n---\n\n## Where PRISM-3D is already ahead and what gaps remain\n\nBased on this competitive survey, the strategic assessment is as follows.\n\n**Advantages PRISM-3D can claim** if it delivers on its architecture: it would be the only code combining 3D geometry with detailed H\u2082 rovibrational physics, the only code with self-consistent 3D dust evolution feedback on gas chemistry, and the first PDR code designed from the ground up for JWST-era spectral predictions at sub-arcsecond resolution. The \"terraced PDR\" discovery means **3D is no longer optional** \u2014 it is required to model the highest-profile observational datasets in the field.\n\n**Critical gaps that must be filled**, in priority order:\n\n- **Self-consistent dust-gas coupling in 3D** \u2014 this is the single highest-impact capability gap. Elyajouri et al.'s 15\u00d7 nano-grain depletion result means every gas-phase prediction (temperature, H\u2082 formation, photoelectric heating, line intensities) is wrong if dust evolution is neglected. No code does this in 3D. Implementing THEMIS-derived grain physics with position-dependent evolution would be an immediate, publishable first.\n- **Full H\u2082 rovibrational treatment in 3D** \u2014 the Meudon code handles this exquisitely in 1D, but extending it to 3D requires solving the UV pumping cascade along multiple directions simultaneously, which is computationally expensive. A pragmatic intermediate step is state-resolved H\u2082 with reduced vibrational levels (v=0\u20133) and full rotational resolution, benchmarked against Meudon in the 1D limit.\n- **Time-dependent dynamics** \u2014 the over-pressurized gas at Horsehead and Orion Bar dissociation fronts points to photoevaporative dynamics as essential physics. Even simple advection of chemical abundances through a prescribed velocity field would go beyond what any current equilibrium PDR code offers.\n- **Large chemical networks compatible with 3D** \u2014 the tension between network size (Meudon: hundreds of species) and 3D computational cost (3D-PDR: 33\u2013128 species) is the fundamental scaling challenge. ML chemistry surrogates trained on Meudon-scale networks but deployed in 3D, using the hybrid approach with timescale-based updates, offer the most promising resolution.\n- **Synthetic observation pipeline** \u2014 PRISM-3D needs an integrated post-processing chain producing JWST-comparable outputs: NIRCam/MIRI photometric images, NIRSpec/MRS spectral cubes, with instrument effects (PSF, noise, spatial sampling) applied. This is the gateway to publication in the PDRs4All ecosystem.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nThe PDR modeling field is ripe for disruption. Five aging codes share the landscape, none designed for the 3D, high-resolution, dust-evolving reality that JWST has revealed. The Meudon code has the best physics but is trapped in 1D; 3D-PDR has the right geometry but stripped-down physics; KOSMA-\u03c4 offers clumpy ensembles but no true 3D; Cloudy has the broadest scope but cannot model spatial structure. PRISM-3D's unique opportunity is the **simultaneous integration of 3D TreeRay-class radiative transfer, THEMIS dust evolution with gas-phase feedback, Meudon-grade H\u2082 microphysics, and ML-accelerated chemistry** \u2014 a combination that would be genuinely unprecedented. The development sequence should prioritize self-consistent 3D dust-gas coupling first (highest scientific impact, clearest competitive gap), followed by H\u2082 rovibrational treatment and JWST synthetic observation capability. The first validation target should be the Orion Bar PDRs4All dataset, where the 3D terraced geometry is already characterized and no existing code can reproduce the full spatial and spectral complexity. Success here would establish PRISM-3D as the reference code for the JWST era.",

PRISM-3D can occupy this.\n\n**JWST has broken 1D models:** The Orion Bar has three terraced dissociation fronts (not one flat surface), nano-grains are depleted 15\u00d7 at the illuminated edge, and the Horsehead shows over-pressurized H\u2082 gas (10\u00d7 thermal pressure of ionized gas) that no equilibrium model explains. These are fundamentally 3D, time-dependent phenomena.\n\n**The dust gap is our #1 priority:** Every competitor either has sophisticated dust in 1D (Meudon+DustEM, KOSMA-\u03c4+MCDRT) or crude dust in 3D (3D-PDR). Nobody does THEMIS-class evolved dust self-consistently coupled to gas chemistry in 3D.\n\n**ML chemistry is promising but fragile:** NeuralPDR, Branca & Pallottini's DeepONet, and Palud's ANN emulators all work for specific use cases but accumulate errors when used iteratively. Our hybrid approach (ML for first guess, physics for refinement) is the right strategy.\n\n## Updated Priority Roadmap\n\n1. **THEMIS dust module** (highest impact, clearest competitive gap)\n2. **H\u2082 rovibrational levels** (essential for JWST line comparison)\n3. **Synthetic JWST observation pipeline** (gateway to PDRs4All comparison)\n4. **Time-dependent advection** (explains over-pressurized gas)\n5. **ML-accelerated chemistry** (enables large 3D grids)\n6. **Bayesian inference framework** (Beetroots-compatible model grids)\n\nWant to start building the THEMIS dust module now? That's the single highest-impact feature \u2014 no competitor has self-consistent 3D dust evolution coupled to gas chemistry, and JWST has proven it matters by a factor of 15\u00d7.",
